title.zh: 卷三十六 楚元王傳
title.en: Volume 36: Prince Yuan of Chu --- Liu Xiang and Liu Xin
title.raw: 卷三十六 楚元王傳 Volume 36: Prince Yuan of Chu --- Liu Xiang and Liu Xin

sentences.zh: 楚元王交字游，高祖同父少弟也。

translations.literal: Prince Yuan of Chu, Liu Jiao, styled You, was Gaozu’s younger brother by the same father.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Jiao—posthumously honored as Prince Yuan of Chu and styled You—was Gaozu’s youngest brother by the same father.

sentences.zh: 好書，多材藝。

translations.literal: He loved writing and had many talents and skills.
translations.idiomatic: He loved books and showed wide gifts.

sentences.zh: 少時嘗與魯穆生、白生、申公俱受詩於浮丘伯。

translations.literal: In his youth he once studied the Odes under Fuqiu Bo together with Mu Sheng of Lu, Bai Sheng, and Shen Gong.
translations.idiomatic: As a young man he read the *Book of Odes* under Fuqiu Bo with Mu Sheng of Lu, Bai Sheng, and Shen Gong.

sentences.zh: 伯者，孫卿門人也。

translations.literal: Bo was a disciple of Sun Qing.
translations.idiomatic: Fuqiu Bo had been a pupil of Xunzi (Sun Qing).

sentences.zh: 及秦焚書，各別去。

translations.literal: When Qin burned the books, they each went their separate ways.
translations.idiomatic: When the Qin ordered the books burned, the scholars scattered.

sentences.zh: 高祖兄弟四人，長兄伯，次仲，伯蚤卒。

translations.literal: Gaozu had four brothers: the eldest was Bo, next Zhong; Bo died early.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu had four brothers: the eldest Bo, then Zhong—Bo died young.

sentences.zh: 高祖既為沛公，景駒自立為楚王。

translations.literal: After Gaozu became lord of Pei, Jing Ju set himself up as king of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Once Gaozu was made lord of Pei, Jing Ju declared himself king of Chu.

sentences.zh: 高祖使仲與審食其留侍太上皇，交與蕭、曹等俱從高祖見景駒，遇項梁，共立楚懷王。

translations.literal: Gaozu ordered Zhong and Shen Yiji to remain and attend the Senior Emperor; Jiao together with Xiao He, Cao Shen, and others all followed Gaozu to see Jing Ju, met Xiang Liang, and together enthroned King Huai of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu left Zhong and Shen Yiji to wait on the Senior Emperor; Jiao marched with Xiao He, Cao Shen, and the rest to join Gaozu before Jing Ju, met Xiang Liang, and helped enthrone King Huai of Chu.

sentences.zh: 因西攻南陽，入武關，與秦戰於藍田。

translations.literal: Thereupon they attacked Nanyang to the west, entered Wu Pass, and fought Qin at Lantian.
translations.idiomatic: They drove west into Nanyang, forced Wu Pass, and met Qin troops at Lantian.

sentences.zh: 至霸上，封交為文信君，從入蜀漢，還定三秦，誅項籍。

translations.literal: Reaching Bashang, he enfeoffed Jiao as lord Wenxin, followed him into Shu and Han, returned to pacify the three Qin, and executed Xiang Ji.
translations.idiomatic: At Bashang he named Jiao lord Wenxin; Jiao followed him into Shu and Han, returned to settle the three Qin, and helped destroy Xiang Yu.

sentences.zh: 即帝位，交與盧綰常侍上，出入臥內，傳言語諸內事隱謀。

translations.literal: When he took the imperial throne, Jiao together with Lu Wan constantly attended the sovereign, entering and leaving the bedchamber, conveying speech and all inner-court affairs and secret plans.
translations.idiomatic: After the accession, Jiao and Lu Wan were constant attendants in the inner chambers, carrying private word and palace secrets.

sentences.zh: 而上從父兄劉賈數別將。

translations.literal: Yet the emperor’s patrilateral cousin Liu Jia was repeatedly sent out as a separate commander.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile Gaozu’s cousin Liu Jia was again and again given independent commands.

sentences.zh: 漢六年，既廢楚王信，分其地為二國，立賈為荊王，交為楚王，王薛郡、東海、彭城三十六縣，先有功也。

translations.literal: In the sixth year of Han, after King Xin of Chu had already been deposed and his land split into two states, he established Jia as king of Jing and Jiao as king of Chu, ruling thirty-six counties of Xue commandery, Donghai, and Pengcheng—because of prior merit.
translations.idiomatic: In Han’s sixth year, with King Xin of Chu removed and his territory halved, Gaozu made Jia king of Jing and Jiao king of Chu over thirty-six counties in Xue, Donghai, and Pengcheng—reward for old service.

sentences.zh: 後封次兄仲為代王，長子肥為齊王。

translations.literal: Later he enfeoffed his second elder brother Zhong as king of Dai and his eldest son Fei as king of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Later Zhong became king of Dai and Gaozu’s eldest son Fei king of Qi.

sentences.zh: 初，高祖微時，常避事，時時與賓客過其丘嫂食。

translations.literal: Earlier, when Gaozu was still obscure, he often fled trouble and from time to time brought guests to his elder brother’s wife’s home for meals.
translations.idiomatic: In Gaozu’s lean years he often dodged trouble and would drop in with guests on his elder sister-in-law for a meal.

sentences.zh: 嫂厭叔與客來，陽為羹盡，轑釜，客以故去。

translations.literal: The sister-in-law detested the uncle’s bringing guests; she feigned that the gruel was gone and scraped the cauldron; the guests therefore left.
translations.idiomatic: She resented her brother-in-law’s crowds and pretended the pot was empty, scraping the kettle with her ladle until the guests took the hint and left.

sentences.zh: 已而視釜中有羹，繇是怨嫂。

translations.literal: Afterward, looking into the cauldron, there was still gruel; on this account he resented his sister-in-law.
translations.idiomatic: When he found stew still in the pot, he nursed a grudge against her.

sentences.zh: 及立齊、代王，而伯子獨不得侯。

translations.literal: When Qi and Dai had been made kingdoms, Bo’s son alone received no marquisate.
translations.idiomatic: Once Qi and Dai were kingdoms, only Bo’s son was left without a fief.

sentences.zh: 太上皇以為言，高祖曰：「某非敢忘封之也，為其母不長者。」

translations.literal: The Senior Emperor spoke on his behalf; Gaozu said, “It is not that I dare forget to enfeoff him—it is because his mother is no elder.”
translations.idiomatic: The Senior Emperor pleaded for the boy; Gaozu replied, “I have not forgotten him—his mother is no matron worthy of the honor.”

sentences.zh: 七年十月，封其子信為羹頡侯。

translations.literal: In the tenth month of the seventh year he enfeoffed his son Xin as marquis of Gengjie.
translations.idiomatic: In the seventh year, tenth month, he enfeoffed the son Xin as marquis of Gengjie—“Scrape-the-Pot.”

sentences.zh: 元王既至楚，以穆生、白生、申公為中大夫。

translations.literal: After Prince Yuan reached Chu, he appointed Mu Sheng, Bai Sheng, and Shen Gong as palace counsellors of the middle rank.
translations.idiomatic: Once Prince Yuan was in Chu, he made Mu Sheng, Bai Sheng, and Shen Gong middle-rank palace counsellors.

sentences.zh: 高后時，浮丘伯在長安，元王遣子郢客與申公俱卒業。

translations.literal: During Empress Gao’s reign Fuqiu Bo was in Chang’an; Prince Yuan sent his son Ying Ke together with Shen Gong to complete their studies.
translations.idiomatic: Under Empress Gao, Fuqiu Bo taught at Chang’an; Yuan sent his son Ying Ke with Shen Gong to finish their schooling.

sentences.zh: 文帝時，聞申公為詩最精，以為博士。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Wen it was heard that Shen Gong was most expert in the Odes, and he was made erudite.
translations.idiomatic: Wendi heard that Shen Gong’s scholarship in the *Odes* was unmatched and appointed him an erudite.

sentences.zh: 元王好詩，諸子皆讀詩，申公始為詩傳，號魯詩。

translations.literal: Prince Yuan loved the Odes; all his sons read the Odes; Shen Gong first composed a commentary on the Odes, called the Lu school.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan loved the *Odes*; every son studied them. Shen Gong wrote the first commentary, the tradition later called Lu *Odes*.

sentences.zh: 元王亦次之詩傳，號曰元王詩，世或有之。

translations.literal: Prince Yuan also composed a commentary on the Odes, styled Prince Yuan’s Odes; copies sometimes survive in the world.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan wrote a commentary too, called the Prince Yuan *Odes*; fragments still circulate.

sentences.zh: 高后時，以元王子郢客為宗正，封上邳侯。

translations.literal: During Empress Gao’s reign Yuan’s son Ying Ke was made director of imperial clan affairs and enfeoffed marquis of Shangpi.
translations.idiomatic: Under Empress Gao, Yuan’s son Ying Ke became director of imperial clan affairs and marquis of Shangpi.

sentences.zh: 元王立二十三年薨，太子辟非先卒，文帝乃以宗正上邳侯郢客嗣，是為夷王。

translations.literal: Prince Yuan reigned twenty-three years and died; the heir Pi Fei had predeceased him, so Wendi had Ying Ke the director and marquis of Shangpi succeed—this was King Yi.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan ruled twenty-three years and died; crown prince Pi Fei had already fallen, so Wendi moved Ying Ke from the directorate to the throne as King Yi.

sentences.zh: 申公為博士，失官，隨郢客歸，復以為中大夫。

translations.literal: Shen Gong, having lost his erudite post, followed Ying Ke home and was again made a middle-rank palace counsellor.
translations.idiomatic: Shen Gong lost his erudite chair, followed Ying Ke back to Chu, and was again named middle counsellor.

sentences.zh: 立四年薨，子戊嗣。

translations.literal: He reigned four years and died; his son Wu succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Yi ruled four years and died; his son Wu inherited.

sentences.zh: 文帝尊寵元王，子生，爵比皇子。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen honored Prince Yuan; when a son was born he granted him noble rank equal to an imperial prince.
translations.idiomatic: Wendi doted on the memory of Prince Yuan: when a son was born to the line, he gave the child rank on a par with the emperor’s own boys.

sentences.zh: 景帝即位，以親親封元王寵子五人：子禮為平陸侯，富為休侯，歲為沈猶侯，埶為宛朐侯，調為棘樂侯。

translations.literal: When Emperor Jing took the throne, out of kinship affection he enfeoffed five of Prince Yuan’s favored sons: Li as marquis of Pinglu, Fu as marquis of Xiu, Sui as marquis of Shenyou, Shi as marquis of Wanqu, and Diao as marquis of Jile.
translations.idiomatic: Jingdi, favoring close kin, enfeoffed five of Yuan’s pampered sons—Li at Pinglu, Fu at Xiu, Sui at Shenyou, Shi at Wanqu, Diao at Jile.

sentences.zh: 王戊稍淫暴，二十年，為薄太后服私姦，削東海、薛郡，乃與吳通謀。

translations.literal: King Wu of Chu, Liu Wu, gradually grew dissolute and cruel; in his twentieth year of rule, while in mourning for Empress Dowager Bo he committed private misconduct; Donghai and Xue were stripped from him, and he then plotted with the king of Wu.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Wu, king of Chu, turned violent and debauched; in his twentieth year on the throne he disgraced himself during mourning for Empress Dowager Bo, lost Donghai and Xue, and then leagued with the king of Wu.

sentences.zh: 二人諫，不聽，胥靡之，衣之赭衣，使杵臼雅舂於市。

translations.literal: The two remonstrated; he would not listen; he sentenced them to penal labor, clothed them in crimson convict dress, and had them pound grain with mortar and pestle in the marketplace as convict labor.
translations.idiomatic: They remonstrated; he ignored them, shaved them for convict labor, dressed them in ochre robes, and set them pounding in the public market.

sentences.zh: 休侯使人諫王，王曰：「季父不吾與，我起，先取季父矣。」

translations.literal: The marquis of Xiu sent someone to remonstrate with the king; the king said, “If my uncle will not side with me, when I rise I shall seize my uncle first.”
translations.idiomatic: Marquis Fu of Xiu sent a warning; Wu answered, “If my uncle will not back me, when I rise he will be the first I take.”

sentences.zh: 休侯懼，乃與母太夫人奔京師。

translations.literal: The marquis of Xiu was afraid and fled with his mother the great lady to the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Fu took fright and bolted to the capital with his mother, the dowager lady.

sentences.zh: 二十一年春，景帝之三年也，削書到，遂應吳王反。

translations.literal: In the spring of the twenty-first year—the third year of Emperor Jing—when the edict of confiscation arrived, he then answered the king of Wu in rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: In spring of his twenty-first year—Jingdi’s third year—the stripping edict arrived and he joined the king of Wu in revolt.

sentences.zh: 其相張尚、太傅趙夷吾諫，不聽。

translations.literal: His chancellor Zhang Shang and grand tutor Zhao Yiwu remonstrated; he would not listen.
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor Zhang Shang and grand tutor Zhao Yiwu pleaded with him; he would not hear them.

sentences.zh: 遂殺尚、夷吾，起兵會吳西攻梁，破棘壁，至昌邑南，與漢將周亞夫戰。

translations.literal: He then killed Shang and Yiwu, raised troops, joined Wu, attacked Liang to the west, broke the rampart at Ji, reached the south of Changyi, and fought Han general Zhou Yafu.
translations.idiomatic: He executed Shang and Yiwu, mobilized, marched with Wu west against Liang, stormed Ji’s wall, reached south of Changyi, and met Zhou Yafu.

sentences.zh: 漢絕吳楚糧道，士饑，吳王走，戊自殺，軍遂降漢。

translations.literal: Han cut Wu and Chu grain lines; the soldiers starved; the king of Wu fled; Wu committed suicide; the army then surrendered to Han.
translations.idiomatic: Han strangled the supply lines; the men went hungry; the king of Wu fled; Liu Wu killed himself; the Chu host capitulated.

sentences.zh: 初，休侯富既奔京師，而王戊反，富等皆坐免侯，削屬籍。

translations.literal: Earlier, after Marquis Fu of Xiu had fled to the capital, when King Wu rebelled, Fu and the others were all stripped of their marquisates and removed from the clan register.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier Fu had reached the capital; when Wu rose, Fu and his kin lost their marquisates and were struck from the roll.

sentences.zh: 後聞其數諫戊，乃更封為紅侯。

translations.literal: Later, hearing that he had repeatedly remonstrated with Wu, he was re-enfeoffed as marquis of Hong.
translations.idiomatic: Learning how often Fu had warned Wu, the court re-enfeoffed him at Hong.

sentences.zh: 太夫人與竇太后有親，懲山東之寇，求留京師，詔許之。

translations.literal: The great lady was kin to Empress Dowager Dou; mindful of the banditry east of the mountains, she begged to remain in the capital; an edict granted it.
translations.idiomatic: The dowager lady was related to Empress Dowager Dou; fearing turmoil in the east, she asked to stay in the capital and was allowed.

sentences.zh: 富子辟彊等四人供養，仕於朝。

translations.literal: Fu’s sons Piqiang and four others supported her and held office at court.
translations.idiomatic: Fu’s son Piqiang and four brothers kept her household and served at court.

sentences.zh: 太夫人薨，賜塋，葬靈戶。

translations.literal: When the great lady died, a burial mound was granted and she was interred at Linghu.
translations.idiomatic: When she died the throne gave her a tomb plot at Linghu.

sentences.zh: 富傳國至曾孫，無子，絕。

translations.literal: Fu’s state passed to a great-grandson; having no son, it was extinguished.
translations.idiomatic: Fu’s line ran to a great-grandson and died out for want of an heir.

sentences.zh: 劉辟彊

translations.literal: Liu Piqiang.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Piqiang.

sentences.zh: 德字路叔少，修黃老術，有智略。

translations.literal: De, styled Lushu—Liu De—in his youth cultivated Huang-Lao techniques and possessed shrewd judgment.
translations.idiomatic: Liu De, courtesy name Lushu, studied Huang–Lao methods from boyhood and was shrewd.

sentences.zh: 少時數言事，召見甘泉宮，武帝謂之「千里駒」。

translations.literal: In his youth he repeatedly spoke on affairs of state, was summoned to audience at Ganquan Palace, and Emperor Wu called him a “thousand-li colt.”
translations.idiomatic: While still young he often spoke on policy; summoned to Ganquan, Wudi called him a “colt that runs a thousand li.”

sentences.zh: 昭帝初，為宗正丞，雜治劉澤詔獄。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Emperor Zhao’s reign he was assistant director of imperial clan affairs and jointly tried the imperial edict case of Liu Ze.
translations.idiomatic: Under Zhaodi he served as assistant director of clan affairs and helped try Liu Ze’s edict case.

sentences.zh: 父為宗正，徙大鴻臚丞，遷太中大夫，後復為宗正，雜案上官氏、蓋主事。

translations.literal: His father had served as director of imperial clan affairs; he was transferred to assistant to the grand herald, promoted grand counsellor of the palace, later again became director of imperial clan affairs, and jointly investigated the cases of the Shangguan clan and the princess of Gai.
translations.idiomatic: His father had been clan director; De rose to assistant grand herald, then grand counsellor of the palace, then clan director again, and helped try the Shangguans and Princess Gai.

sentences.zh: 德常持老子知足之計。

translations.literal: De constantly held to Laozi’s teaching of knowing contentment.
translations.idiomatic: De lived by Laozi’s counsel to know when enough is enough.

sentences.zh: 妻死，大將軍光欲以女妻之，德不敢取，畏盛滿也。

translations.literal: When his wife died, Grand General Huo Guang wished to give him his daughter in marriage; De dared not take her, fearing the peril of excess.
translations.idiomatic: When his wife died, Huo Guang offered a daughter; De declined, wary of riding too high.

sentences.zh: 蓋長公主孫譚遮德自言，德數責以公主起居無狀。

translations.literal: The princess of Gai’s grandson Tan intercepted De and spoke for himself; De repeatedly rebuked him that the princess’s conduct was improper.
translations.idiomatic: Gai’s grandson Tan waylaid De to plead; De scolded him again for the princess’s scandalous behavior.

sentences.zh: 侍御史以為光望不受女，承指劾德誹謗詔獄，免為庶人，屏居山田。

translations.literal: The attendant secretary, thinking that Guang resented De’s refusal of the daughter, seized the occasion to impeach De for slander and had him sent to edict prison; he was dismissed as commoner and lived in seclusion on mountain land.
translations.idiomatic: An attendant secretary, guessing Guang’s pique at the refused match, impeached De for slander; he fell to commoner status and withdrew to a farm.

sentences.zh: 光聞而恨之，復白召德守青州刺史。

translations.literal: When Guang heard of it he resented this, then again memorialized to summon De to serve as governor of Qingzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Guang heard, smarted, then had him recalled as governor of Qingzhou.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，復為宗正，與立宣帝，以定策賜爵關內侯。

translations.literal: After more than a year he was again director of imperial clan affairs, took part in enthroning Emperor Xuan, and for fixing the succession was granted noble rank within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: A year later he was clan director again, helped raise Xuandi to the throne, and earned a marquisate-within-the-passes for that counsel.

sentences.zh: 地節中，以親親行謹厚封為陽城侯。

translations.literal: In the Dijie period, out of kinship affection and for prudent conduct he was enfeoffed marquis of Yangcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Mid-Dijie, for kinship and steady virtue he was made marquis of Yangcheng.

sentences.zh: 子安民為郎中右曹，宗家以德得官宿衛者二十餘人。

translations.literal: His son Anmin was gentleman of the palace in the right bureau; more than twenty of the clan kin gained office in the guard through De’s standing.
translations.idiomatic: Son Anmin served in the palace right bureau; over twenty kinsmen won guard posts through De’s influence.

sentences.zh: 向字子政，本名更生。

translations.literal: Xiang, styled Zheng, originally bore the name Gengsheng.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xiang, style Zheng, was first named Gengsheng.

sentences.zh: 年十二，以父德任為輦郎。

translations.literal: At twelve, through his father De’s position, he was appointed gentleman of the palanquin.
translations.idiomatic: At twelve he entered the palanquin corps on his father’s privilege.

sentences.zh: 既冠，以行修飭擢為諫大夫。

translations.literal: After the capping ceremony, for correct conduct he was promoted remonstrant grandee.
translations.idiomatic: After capping, his sober conduct won him promotion to remonstrant grandee.

sentences.zh: 是時，宣帝循武帝故事，招選名儒俊材置左右。

translations.literal: At this time Emperor Xuan followed Emperor Wu’s precedent, summoning choice Confucian scholars and outstanding talents to attend him.
translations.idiomatic: Xuandi, copying Wudi’s habit, called famous scholars and sharp men to court.

sentences.zh: 更生以通達能屬文辭，與王褒、張子僑等並進對，獻賦頌凡數十篇。

translations.literal: Gengsheng, because he was conversant and could compose literary pieces, advanced together with Wang Bao, Zhang Ziqiao, and others in paired audiences, presenting several tens of fu and eulogies.
translations.idiomatic: Gengsheng’s fluency and prose earned him joint audiences with Wang Bao, Zhang Ziqiao, and others; he offered dozens of rhapsodies and hymns.

sentences.zh: 上復興神僊方術之事，而淮南有枕中鴻寶苑秘書。

translations.literal: The sovereign again promoted matters of transcendents and esoteric arts, while in Huainan there was the pillow-casket Hongbao and Garden secret writ.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor revived immortality lore; Huainan held the pillow-sealed *Hongbao* and garden esoterica.

sentences.zh: 書言神僊使鬼物為金之術，及鄒衍重道延命方，世人莫見，而更生父德武帝時治淮南獄得其書。

translations.literal: The book spoke of arts whereby transcendents and ghosts turn things to gold, as well as Zou Yan’s doubled way and life-extending formulae; the world had not seen them, yet Gengsheng’s father De, when he tried the Huainan case under Emperor Wu, obtained this book.
translations.idiomatic: It promised spirits, ghosts, and transmutation to gold, with Zou Yan’s “double path” and longevity recipes—rare texts De had seized when he judged Huainan’s case under Wudi.

sentences.zh: 更生幼而讀誦，以為奇，獻之，言黃金可成。

translations.literal: Gengsheng read and recited it in childhood, thought it marvelous, presented it, and said gold could be forged.
translations.idiomatic: Gengsheng had memorized it as a boy, thought it wonder-working, presented it, and claimed gold could be made.

sentences.zh: 上令典尚方鑄作事，費甚多，方不驗。

translations.literal: The sovereign ordered him to supervise the work of the imperial foundry; expense was very great and the method did not prove out.
translations.idiomatic: The throne put him over the imperial foundries; costs soared and nothing came of the recipe.

sentences.zh: 上乃下更生吏，吏劾更生鑄偽黃金，繫當死。

translations.literal: The sovereign then handed Gengsheng to the officials; the officials impeached Gengsheng for casting false gold; he was bound and liable for death.
translations.idiomatic: He was handed to the law; they charged him with counterfeit gold and bound him for execution.

sentences.zh: 更生兄陽城侯安民上書，入國戶半，贖更生罪。

translations.literal: Gengsheng’s elder brother the marquis of Yangcheng, Anmin, submitted a memorial offering half his fief’s households to ransom Gengsheng’s crime.
translations.idiomatic: His brother Anmin, marquis of Yangcheng, offered half his fief’s households to redeem him.

sentences.zh: 上亦奇其材，得踰冬減死論。

translations.literal: The sovereign also thought his talent extraordinary and, crossing winter, commuted the sentence to spared death.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor valued his gifts and, after he survived winter in jail, spared his life.

sentences.zh: 會初立穀梁春秋，徵更生受穀梁，講論五經於石渠。

translations.literal: It happened that the Guliang *Spring and Autumn* had just been established; Gengsheng was summoned to receive the Guliang and expound the five classics at Stone Canal.
translations.idiomatic: As the new Guliang *Spring and Autumn* chair opened, he was summoned to master it and debate the five classics at the Stone Canal conference.

sentences.zh: 復拜為郎中給事黃門，遷散騎諫大夫給事中。

translations.literal: He was again appointed gentleman of the palace serving at the Yellow Gate, promoted attendant cavalry remonstrant grandee serving within.
translations.idiomatic: He was restored as gentleman at the Yellow Gate, then attendant cavalry remonstrant grandee in attendance.

sentences.zh: 竊聞故前將軍蕭望之等，皆忠正無私，欲致大治，忤於貴戚尚書。

translations.literal: I have heard that the former general Xiao Wangzhi and others were loyal and upright without private bias, wishing to bring about great order, but offended powerful kin and the secretaries of the masters of writing.
translations.idiomatic: I hear the late general Xiao Wangzhi and his like were straight as a line and sought good rule, yet crossed the great families and palace secretaries.

sentences.zh: 今道路人聞望之等復進，以為且復見毀讒，必曰嘗有過之臣不宜復用，是大不然。

translations.literal: Now people on the roads hear that Wangzhi and the others are to be advanced again, and suppose they will soon again be slandered and destroyed; they are sure to say that ministers who once had faults ought not be reused—this is greatly not so.
translations.idiomatic: Word on the street is that Wangzhi’s party will return—and will be torn down again; folk will say disgraced men must never be used again. That is dead wrong.

sentences.zh: 臣聞春秋地震，為在位執政太盛也，不為三獨夫動，亦已明矣。

translations.literal: I have heard that in the *Spring and Autumn* earthquakes mean those in power and holding office are too strong; it is not that they move for three lone men—this is already clear.
translations.idiomatic: The *Spring and Autumn* blames earthquakes on overmighty ministers, not on three isolated men—that lesson is plain.

sentences.zh: 且往者高皇帝時，季布有罪，至於夷滅，後赦以為將軍，高后、孝文之間卒為名臣。

translations.literal: Moreover, in former times under the August Emperor Gao, Ji Bu had a crime reaching even to extinction of his line; later he was pardoned and made a general, and between Empress Gao and Emperor Xiaowen he ended as a famous minister.
translations.idiomatic: Remember Gaozu: Ji Bu faced clan extinction, was pardoned, made a general, and between Lü and Wendi became a statesman of renown.

sentences.zh: 孝武帝時兒寬有重罪繫，按道侯韓說諫曰：「前吾丘壽王死，陛下至今恨之；

translations.literal: Under Emperor Xiaowu, Ni Kuan was bound on a grave charge; Marquis Andao Han Shuo remonstrated, saying, “Earlier when Wuqiu Shouwang died, down to today Your Majesty still regrets it;
translations.idiomatic: Under Wudi, Ni Kuan sat in heavy bonds; Han Shuo of Andao warned, “When Wuqiu Shouwang died you still ache for it;

sentences.zh: 今殺寬，後將復大恨矣！」

translations.literal: if you now kill Kuan, later you will again have great regret!”
translations.idiomatic: kill Kuan now and a deeper regret will follow!”

sentences.zh: 上感其言，遂貰寬，復用之，位至御史大夫，御史大夫未有及寬者也。

translations.literal: The sovereign was moved by his words, thereupon spared Kuan, employed him again, and he rose to grandee secretary—none of the grandee secretaries matched Kuan.
translations.idiomatic: Moved, the emperor spared Kuan, reused him, and raised him to grandee secretary—none ever outshone him.

sentences.zh: 又董仲舒坐私為災異書，主父偃取奏之，下吏，罪至不道，幸蒙不誅，復為太中大夫，膠西相，以老病免歸。

translations.literal: Further, Dong Zhongshu sat for privately composing a book on portents and anomalies; Zhufu Yan seized and memorialized it; he was handed to officials with guilt reaching lack of the Way; fortunately he was not executed, and again became grand counsellor of the palace and chancellor of Jiaoxi, then retired old and ill.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhongshu drafted illicit portent texts; Zhufu Yan seized them; he faced capital charges yet lived, served again as grand counsellor and Jiaoxi chancellor, then retired ill.

sentences.zh: 漢有所欲興，常有詔問。

translations.literal: Whenever Han wished to undertake something, there were always edicts asking him.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever the Han meant to launch a policy, edicts asked his mind.

sentences.zh: 仲舒為世儒宗，定議有益天下。

translations.literal: Zhongshu was the Confucian patriarch of the age; his settled opinions benefited the realm.
translations.idiomatic: He was the age’s Confucian patriarch; his judgments helped the empire.

sentences.zh: 孝宣皇帝時，夏侯勝坐誹謗繫獄，三年免為庶人。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Xiaoxuan, Xiahou Sheng sat in prison for slander; after three years he was released as commoner.
translations.idiomatic: Under Xuandi, Xiahou Sheng sat in jail for slander three years, then was cut to commoner.

sentences.zh: 宣帝復用勝，至長信少府，太子太傅，名敢直言，天下美之。

translations.literal: Emperor Xuan again employed Sheng to reach chamberlain for the palace of enduring faith, grand tutor to the heir, famed for daring to speak straight—the realm praised him.
translations.idiomatic: Xuandi recalled him to chamberlain of Changxin, tutor to the heir, famous for blunt speech—the world admired him.

sentences.zh: 若乃群臣，多此比類，難一二記。

translations.literal: As for the host of ministers, many are of this sort; it is hard to record them one or two.
translations.idiomatic: The roll of such ministers is long; two examples cannot exhaust it.

sentences.zh: 有過之臣，無負國家，有益天下，此四臣者，足以觀矣。

translations.literal: Ministers who had faults yet did not betray the state and benefited the realm—these four subjects are enough to judge by.
translations.idiomatic: Men who stumbled yet never betrayed the throne and still served the common good—these four prove the rule.

sentences.zh: 書奏，恭、顯疑其更生所為，白請考姦詐。

translations.literal: When the memorial was submitted, Hong and Xian suspected it was Gengsheng’s work and reported asking to examine him for deceit.
translations.idiomatic: When the memorial went up, Shi Xian and Hong Gong guessed Gengsheng and asked for an inquest.

sentences.zh: 辭果服，遂逮更生繫獄，下太傅韋玄成、諫大夫貢禹，與廷尉雜考。

translations.literal: Under interrogation he indeed confessed; thereupon Gengsheng was seized and imprisoned, and Grand Tutor Wei Xuancheng, Remonstrant Grandee Gong Yu, together with the commandant of justice jointly examined him.
translations.idiomatic: He confessed; Gengsheng was jailed while Grand Tutor Wei Xuancheng, Remonstrant Gong Yu, and the commandant of justice sat in joint trial.

sentences.zh: 劾更生前為九卿，坐與望之、堪謀排車騎將軍高、許、史氏侍中者，毀離親戚，欲退去之，而獨專權。

translations.literal: They impeached Gengsheng’s earlier service among the nine ministers: sitting in plot with Wangzhi and Kan to squeeze out the chariot-and-cavalry general Gao and the Xu and Shi masters of writing serving within, slandering and alienating imperial kin, wishing to drive them off and monopolize power.
translations.idiomatic: They charged his old nine-minister rank, alleging plot with Wangzhi and Zhou Kan to oust General of Chariots and Cavalry Gao and the Xu and Shi palace favorites, slandering kin to clear the field for themselves.

sentences.zh: 為臣不忠，幸不伏誅，復蒙恩徵用，不悔前過，而教令人言變事，誣罔不道。

translations.literal: As a minister he was disloyal; though fortunately he was not executed, he was again favored and summoned, did not repent his former faults, yet taught others to speak of portents—deceitful and lacking the Way.
translations.idiomatic: Disloyal minister, spared once, favored again, unrepentant, coaching others on omens—rank deceit.

sentences.zh: 更生坐免為庶人。

translations.literal: Gengsheng sat stripped to commoner.
translations.idiomatic: Gengsheng was reduced to commoner.

sentences.zh: 而望之亦坐使子上書自冤前事，恭、顯白令詣獄置對。

translations.literal: Wangzhi also sat for having his son submit a memorial pleading his own innocence in the earlier affair; Hong and Xian reported ordering him to come to prison for confrontation.
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi too fell for letting his son plead his old case; Hong and Xian had him summoned to jail for questioning.

sentences.zh: 望之自殺。

translations.literal: Wangzhi committed suicide.
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi killed himself.

sentences.zh: 天子甚悼恨之，乃擢周堪為光祿勳，堪弟子張猛光祿大夫給事中，大見信任。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven deeply mourned and resented it; he then promoted Zhou Kan to superintendent of the palace, Kan’s disciple Zhang Meng to grand counsellor of the palace serving within, and greatly trusted them.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor grieved; he raised Zhou Kan to superintendent of the palace and Kan’s pupil Zhang Meng to grand counsellor in attendance—both were trusted.

sentences.zh: 恭、顯憚之，數譖毀焉。

translations.literal: Hong and Xian feared them and repeatedly slandered them.
translations.idiomatic: Hong and Xian feared the pair and kept whispering poison.

sentences.zh: 更生見堪、猛在位，幾己得復進，懼其傾危，乃上封事諫曰：

translations.literal: When Gengsheng saw Kan and Meng in office, thinking he himself might soon advance again, he feared they would be overturned, and therefore submitted a sealed memorial remonstrating, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Seeing Kan and Meng in power, hoping for his own recall yet fearing a fall, he filed a sealed remonstrance:

sentences.zh: 臣前幸得以骨肉備九卿，奉法不謹，乃復蒙恩。

translations.literal: Your servant formerly had the fortune, as bone-and-flesh kin, to fill one of the nine ministers; I was not careful in observing the law, yet again received favor.
translations.idiomatic: I once held a nine-minister seat as imperial kin; I broke the rules yet was forgiven.

sentences.zh: 竊見災異並起，天地失常，徵表為國。

translations.literal: Privately I see portents and anomalies arising together, heaven and earth losing their normality, omens declaring themselves for the state.
translations.idiomatic: Portents cluster; heaven and earth are out of joint—signs meant for the throne.

sentences.zh: 欲終不言，念忠臣雖在甽畝，猶不忘君，惓惓之義也。

translations.literal: I wished in the end not to speak, yet I reflect that a loyal minister, though in the ditches of the fields, still does not forget his lord—this is the meaning of earnest devotion.
translations.idiomatic: I meant to stay silent, yet loyal men in the furrow still think of their lord—that is devotion.

sentences.zh: 況重以骨肉之親，又加以舊恩未報乎！

translations.literal: How much more when the tie is bone-and-flesh kin, and old favor yet unrepaid!
translations.idiomatic: How much more when the bond is blood and old kindness unpaid!

sentences.zh: 欲竭愚誠，又恐越職，然惟二恩未報，忠臣之義，一杼愚意，退就農畝，死無所恨。

translations.literal: I wish to exhaust my foolish loyalty, yet fear overstepping my office; still only the two kindnesses I have received remain unrepaid—the duty of a loyal minister—once to set forth my foolish thoughts, then retire to the farming fields: I would die without regret.
translations.idiomatic: I ache to speak plainly yet fear crossing my rank—but two kindnesses are still unpaid, and a loyal man owes this much: I lay out my foolish mind once, then may go back to the plough and meet death content.

sentences.zh: 臣聞舜命九官，濟濟相讓，和之至也。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that when Shun appointed the nine officers, they thronged in dense array yielding one to another—this was harmony at its utmost.
translations.idiomatic: Tradition says Shun’s nine ministers filled the hall deferring to one another—that was harmony perfected.

sentences.zh: 眾賢和於朝，則萬物和於野。

translations.literal: When many worthies are harmonious at court, then the myriad things are harmonious in the wilds.
translations.idiomatic: When good men pull together at court, the world beyond the walls falls into tune.

sentences.zh: 故簫韶九成，而鳳皇來儀；

translations.literal: Therefore when the Xiaoshao was played to the ninth completion, the phoenixes came with measured gait;
translations.idiomatic: Play the Ninefold Shao to its close and phoenixes arrive in measured step;

sentences.zh: 擊石拊石，百獸率舞。

translations.literal: striking and tapping the stone chimes, the hundred beasts led one another in dance.
translations.idiomatic: strike the lithophones and every beast sways in time.

sentences.zh: 四海之內，靡不和寧。

translations.literal: Within the four seas there was none who was not harmonious and at peace.
translations.idiomatic: Under heaven none stood outside that peace.

sentences.zh: 及至周文，開基西郊，雜遝眾賢，罔不肅和，崇推讓之風，以銷分爭之訟。

translations.literal: Down to King Wen of Zhou, who opened the foundation west of the capital, thronging worthies gathered, none not solemn and harmonious; he honored the wind of yielding deference to quell lawsuits of mutual quarrel.
translations.idiomatic: Then came King Wen, founding west of the capital; worthies flocked in, all grave and at one; he prized courtesy until private quarrels died away.

sentences.zh: 文王既沒，周公思慕，歌詠文王之德，其《詩》曰：「於穆清廟，肅雍顯相；

translations.literal: When King Wen was gone, the Duke of Zhou longed for him and sang of King Wen’s virtue; in the *Odes* it says: “Oh solemn the clear temple, harmonious and grave the glorious helpers;
translations.idiomatic: After Wen died the Duke of Zhou mourned him in song: “How august the bright temple, how stately those who assist;

sentences.zh: 濟濟多士，秉文之德。」

translations.literal: in dense array many officers, upholding Wen’s virtue.”
translations.idiomatic: how thick the host of officers who bear King Wen’s virtue.”

sentences.zh: 當此之時，武王、周公繼政，朝臣和於內，萬國驩於外，故盡得其驩心，以事其先祖。

translations.literal: At that time King Wu and the Duke of Zhou succeeded in government; court ministers were harmonious within, the myriad states joyful without; therefore they fully won joyful hearts to serve their founding ancestors.
translations.idiomatic: Wu and the Duke of Zhou then ruled: harmony within the hall, joy among the states, so every heart gladly served the shrines.

sentences.zh: 其《詩》曰：「有來雍雍，至止肅肅，相維辟公，天子穆穆。」

translations.literal: The *Odes* says: “They came in harmonious majesty; they halted in solemn awe; assisting were the lords and dukes; the Son of Heaven was august in dignity.”
translations.idiomatic: The *Odes* adds: “They came in solemn joy, they halted in awe; the great lords stood by; the Son of Heaven shone in still majesty.”

sentences.zh: 言四方皆以和來也。

translations.literal: This means the four quarters all came in harmony.
translations.idiomatic: It means the corners of the realm arrived in concord.

sentences.zh: 諸侯和於下，天應報於上，故周頌曰「降福穰穰」，又曰「飴我釐麰」。

translations.literal: The princes being harmonious below, Heaven responded and rewarded above; therefore the Zhou hymns say, “Blessings descend in rich profusion,” and again, “Grant us black and yellow grain.”
translations.idiomatic: Feudal lords kept peace below, Heaven answered above; the Zhou hymns cry, “Blessings shower thick,” and “Feed us black and yellow grain.”

sentences.zh: 釐麰，麥也，始自天降。

translations.literal: “Black and yellow grain” is wheat; it first descended from Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Those grains are wheat—Heaven’s first gift.

sentences.zh: 此皆以和致和，獲天助也。

translations.literal: All these are cases of using harmony to bring harmony and obtaining Heaven’s aid.
translations.idiomatic: Harmony called forth harmony and drew Heaven’s help.

sentences.zh: 下至幽、厲之際，朝廷不和，轉相非怨，詩人疾而憂之曰：「民之無良，相怨一方。」

translations.literal: Down to the age of Kings You and Li, the court was not harmonious, turning to blame and resentment; the poets, sick and grieved, said, “The people are not good, blaming one another on their own side.”
translations.idiomatic: Under You and Li the court split; blame flew; the poets cried, “The people turn vicious, each camp cursing the other.”

sentences.zh: 眾小在位而從邪議，歙歙相是而背君子，故其《詩》曰：「歙歙訿訿，亦孔之哀！

translations.literal: Many petty men held office and followed wicked counsel; fawning they approved one another and turned their backs on gentlemen; therefore the *Odes* says, “Fawning, whispering—how utterly piteous!
translations.idiomatic: Petty men took office, flattered wicked plans, hissed approval, and shunned gentlemen—so the *Odes* wails, “Whisper, flatter—what grief!

sentences.zh: 謀之其臧，則具是違；

translations.literal: When the plan is good, they all oppose it;
translations.idiomatic: Call a good plan—they spurn it;

sentences.zh: 謀之不臧，則具是依！」

translations.literal: when the plan is not good, they all adhere to it!”
translations.idiomatic: call a bad plan—they cling to it!”

sentences.zh: 君子獨處守正，不橈眾枉，勉彊以從王事則反見憎毒讒愬，故其《詩》曰：「密勿從事，不敢告勞，無罪無辜，讒口嗷嗷！」

translations.literal: Gentlemen alone kept to the right, not bending to the crowd’s wrong; straining to follow the king’s business they were instead met with hatred, poison, and slanderous accusation; therefore the *Odes* says, “Diligently I serve, yet dare not tell my toil; guiltless and faultless, slanderous mouths cry aloud!”
translations.idiomatic: Good men stood straight, refused to bow to the crooked, and for serving the king earned only venom and suit—so the *Odes* says, “I toil in silence, dare not boast my labor; I am blameless, yet slander shouts.”

sentences.zh: 當是之時，日月薄蝕而無光，其《詩》曰：「朔日辛卯，日有蝕之，亦孔之醜！」

translations.literal: At that time sun and moon were eclipsed and without light; the *Odes* says, “On the first day, xinmao, there was a solar eclipse—how very shameful!”
translations.idiomatic: Then sun and moon went dark; the *Odes* records, “On xinmao, the new moon’s day—the sun was eaten—what shame!”

sentences.zh: 又曰：「彼月而微，此日而微，今此下民，亦孔之哀！」

translations.literal: Again it says, “That moon was faint; this sun was faint; now these lower people—how very piteous!”
translations.idiomatic: Again: “That moon thinned, this sun thinned—oh the pity of the people below!”

sentences.zh: 又曰：「日月鞠凶，不用其行；

translations.literal: Again it says, “Sun and moon gather calamity, not keeping to their courses;
translations.idiomatic: Again: “Sun and moon hoard disaster, leave their tracks;

sentences.zh: 四國無政，不用其良！」

translations.literal: the four states lack good government, not employing their good men!”
translations.idiomatic: the four quarters lose the way, spurn their good men!”

sentences.zh: 天變見於上，地變動於下，水泉沸騰，山谷易處。

translations.literal: Heaven’s changes appeared above; earth’s changes stirred below; springs boiled over; hills and valleys exchanged places.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven showed prodigy above, earth shuddered below, springs seethed, ridges sank into valleys.

sentences.zh: 其《詩》曰：「百川沸騰，山冢卒崩，高岸為谷，深谷為陵。

translations.literal: The *Odes* says, “The hundred streams boil; mountain peaks topple; high banks become valleys; deep valleys become ridges.
translations.idiomatic: The *Odes* says, “Every river boils, peak tombs collapse, high shores turn to gullies, deep gullies turn to hills,”

sentences.zh: 哀今之人，胡憯莫懲！」

translations.literal: Pity the men of today—why is there none to set them aright!”
translations.idiomatic: and pity today’s men—who will call them back?”

sentences.zh: 霜降失節，不以其時，其《詩》曰：「正月繁霜，我心憂傷；

translations.literal: Frost fell out of season, not in its proper time; the *Odes* says, “In the first month, thick frost—my heart grieves;
translations.idiomatic: Frost fell in the wrong month; the *Odes* says, “First month, frost in drifts—my heart breaks;

sentences.zh: 民之訛言，亦孔之將！」

translations.literal: the people’s false rumors—how very mighty they grow!”
translations.idiomatic: the people’s lies—how huge they swell!”

sentences.zh: 言民以是為非，甚眾大也。

translations.literal: This means the people took wrong for right—very many and great.
translations.idiomatic: It means the crowd called evil good, and the cry grew vast.

sentences.zh: 此皆不和，賢不肖易位之所致也。

translations.literal: All this came from lack of harmony and the worthy and unworthy exchanging places.
translations.idiomatic: All stemmed from discord and good men trading seats with knaves.

sentences.zh: 由此觀之，和氣致祥，乖氣致異；

translations.literal: From this it may be seen: harmonizing qi brings auspice; contrary qi brings strangeness;
translations.idiomatic: So concord draws blessing; perversity draws wonder;

sentences.zh: 祥多者其國安，異眾者其國危，天地之常經，古今之通義也。

translations.literal: where auspices are many the state is secure; where anomalies are many the state is in peril—Heaven and Earth’s constant rule, a principle valid from antiquity to today.
translations.idiomatic: many omens mean a steady realm, many prodigies a tottering one—Heaven’s old law, true in every age.

sentences.zh: 今陛下開三代之業，招文學之士，優游寬容，使得並進。

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty has opened the enterprise of the three ages, summoned men of letters and learning, been leisurely and tolerant, allowing them to advance together.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty has reopened the work of the three dynasties, welcomed scholars, and let them rise in patient ease.

sentences.zh: 今賢不肖渾殽，白黑不分，邪正雜糅，忠讒並進。

translations.literal: Now worthy and unworthy are mixed in confusion, white and black not distinguished, wrong and right tangled together, loyal and slanderous advancing together.
translations.idiomatic: Yet worthy and base are jumbled, black and white blurred, crooked and straight tangled, loyal words and slander ride abreast.

sentences.zh: 章交公車，人滿北軍。

translations.literal: Memorials crowd the public carriage gate; men pack the Northern Army.
translations.idiomatic: Petitions choke the carriage gate; the Northern Army swarms with appellants.

sentences.zh: 朝臣舛午，膠戾乘剌，更相讒愬，轉相是非。

translations.literal: Court ministers oppose one another crosswise; tangled malice rides on spite; they take turns slandering and accusing, shifting blame on one another.
translations.idiomatic: Ministers face off at angles; spite finds a perch; they trade indictments and spin each other false.

sentences.zh: 傳授增加，文書紛糾，前後錯繆，毀譽渾亂。

translations.literal: Transmission and instruction multiply; documents swarm confused; front and rear are tangled wrong; praise and blame are jumbled.
translations.idiomatic: Teachings multiply, files tangle, early and late contradict, praise and blame blur.

sentences.zh: 所以熒惑耳目，感移心意，不可勝載。

translations.literal: By these means they dazzled ears and eyes and shifted hearts and minds—more than can be fully set down.
translations.idiomatic: Thus they dazzled eye and ear, tugged the ruler’s heart, beyond any ledger’s count.

sentences.zh: 分曹為黨，往往群朋，將同心以陷正臣。

translations.literal: Splitting into factions they formed cliques; constantly in gangs and circles, intending with one mind to trap upright ministers.
translations.idiomatic: They split into bureaus, built cliques, flocked in packs, and meant to ruin straight men as one.

sentences.zh: 正臣進者，治之表也；

translations.literal: When upright ministers advance, that is the sign of order;
translations.idiomatic: Straight ministers rising mark good rule;

sentences.zh: 正臣陷者，亂之機也。

translations.literal: when upright ministers are trapped, that is the hinge of chaos.
translations.idiomatic: straight ministers trapped marks the hinge of ruin.

sentences.zh: 乘治亂之機，未知孰任，而災異數見，此臣所以寒心者也。

translations.literal: Riding the hinge of order and chaos, not knowing whom to entrust, yet portents appear repeatedly—this is why your servant’s heart grows cold.
translations.idiomatic: Stand at that hinge unsure whom to trust while omens pile up—your servant’s blood runs cold.

sentences.zh: 夫乘權藉勢之人，子弟鱗集於朝，羽翼陰附者眾，輻湊於前，毀譽將必用，以終乖離之咎。

translations.literal: Those who ride power and lean on influence—sons and younger kin swarm the court like scales; many secretly attach as wings; they converge like spokes at the hub; praise and blame they will surely use, to finish in the calamity of estrangement.
translations.idiomatic: Men who clutch power breed sons and clients like fish scales, hide allies in shadow, crowd the ruler’s mat, trade slander and praise, and end in bitter split.

sentences.zh: 是以日月無光，雪霜夏隕，海水沸出，陵谷易處，列星失行，皆怨氣之所致也。

translations.literal: Therefore sun and moon lose their light, snow and frost fall in summer, the sea boils over, hills and valleys change place, the arrayed stars miss their courses—all are brought about by miasmic qi of resentment.
translations.idiomatic: Hence the lamps of sky go dim, frost falls in summer, seas boil, hills trade places, stars wander—all from choked resentment below.

sentences.zh: 夫遵衰周之軌跡，循詩人之所刺，而欲以成太平，致雅頌，猶卻行而求及前人也。

translations.literal: To follow the tracks of declining Zhou, tread what the poets satirized, yet wish to accomplish great peace and attain the Ya and Song odes—is like walking backward to catch someone ahead.
translations.idiomatic: Trace late Zhou’s rut, repeat what the *Odes* condemn, yet hope for peace and hymns of praise—like walking backward to overtake a man ahead.

sentences.zh: 初元以來六年矣，案春秋六年之中，災異未有稠如今者也。

translations.literal: Six years have passed since Chuyuan; checking the six years within the *Spring and Autumn*, anomalies were never so thick as now.
translations.idiomatic: Six years since Chuyuan: in any six years the *Spring and Autumn* records, prodigies never clustered this thick.

sentences.zh: 夫有春秋之異，無孔子之救，猶不能解紛，況甚於春秋乎？

translations.literal: To have the *Spring and Autumn*’s anomalies without Confucius to save them still could not untangle confusion—how much less when it exceeds the *Spring and Autumn*?
translations.idiomatic: Those ages had omens yet lacked Confucius to mend them—still confusion reigned; how much worse when omens outrun the classic?

sentences.zh: 原其所以然者，讒邪並進也。

translations.literal: Tracing why it is so: slanderous evil advances together.
translations.idiomatic: The root is slander rising in packs.

sentences.zh: 讒邪之所以並進者，由上多疑心，既已用賢人而行善政，如或譖之，則賢人退而善政還。

translations.literal: The reason slanderous evil advances together is that above there is much doubtful heart; having already employed worthy men and carried out good government, if someone defames them, then worthy men withdraw and good government reverts.
translations.idiomatic: It rises because the throne doubts: worthies win trust and good rule begins, then a whisper strikes and worthies fall and good policy snaps back.

sentences.zh: 夫執狐疑之心者，來讒賊之口；

translations.literal: He who holds a doubting heart invites slanderous mouths;
translations.idiomatic: Doubt in the breast invites slander at the lip;

sentences.zh: 持不斷之意者，開群枉之門。

translations.literal: he who maintains indecision opens the gate to many wrongs.
translations.idiomatic: hesitation in the hand opens the gate to every crooked thing.

sentences.zh: 讒邪進則眾賢退，群枉盛則正士消。

translations.literal: When slanderous evil advances, many worthies withdraw; when many wrongs flourish, upright gentlemen vanish.
translations.idiomatic: Slander rises and good men fade; wrong swells and straight men disappear.

sentences.zh: 故易有否泰。

translations.literal: Therefore the *Changes* have Pi and Tai.
translations.idiomatic: So the *Changes* pair Obstruction and Peace.

sentences.zh: 小人道長，君子道消，君子道消，則政日亂，故為否。

translations.literal: The petty man’s way grows; the gentleman’s way wanes; when the gentleman’s way wanes, then government daily falls into disorder—therefore it is Pi.
translations.idiomatic: Petty men wax, gentlemen wane; gentlemen wane and rule rots daily—that is Obstruction.

sentences.zh: 否者，閉而亂也。

translations.literal: Pi means closed and disordered.
translations.idiomatic: Obstruction is closure and chaos.

sentences.zh: 君子道長，小人道消，小人道消，則政日治，故為泰。

translations.literal: The gentleman’s way grows; the petty man’s way wanes; when the petty man’s way wanes, then government daily goes toward order—therefore it is Tai.
translations.idiomatic: Gentlemen wax, petty men wane; petty men wane and order grows daily—that is Peace.

sentences.zh: 泰者，通而治也。

translations.literal: Tai means open and ordered.
translations.idiomatic: Peace is openness and calm.

sentences.zh: 《詩》又云「雨雪麃麃，見晛聿消」，與易同義。

translations.literal: The *Odes* also says, “Snow falls thick; when the sun appears it melts”—with the *Changes* it shares the meaning.
translations.idiomatic: The *Odes* adds, “Snow falls thick, yet sun melts it”—the same lesson as the *Changes*.

sentences.zh: 昔者鯀、共工、驩兜與舜、禹雜處堯朝，周公與管、蔡並居周位，當是時，迭進相毀，流言相謗，豈可勝道哉！

translations.literal: Of old Gun, Gonggong, and Huandou were mixed with Shun and Yu at Yao’s court; the Duke of Zhou shared Zhou’s seats with Guan and Cai—in such times they advanced in turns, destroying one another; rumor and slander flew—how can it all be told!
translations.idiomatic: Once Gun, Gonggong, and Huandou jostled Shun and Yu at Yao’s court; the Duke of Zhou sat with Guan and Cai—then rivals climbed over one another, rumor flew—who could count the tales?

sentences.zh: 帝堯、成王能賢舜、禹、周公而消共工、管、蔡，故以大治，榮華至今。

translations.literal: Emperor Yao and King Cheng could deem Shun, Yu, and the Duke of Zhou worthy and dispel Gonggong and Guan and Cai; therefore there was great order, and glory lasts to this day.
translations.idiomatic: Yao and Cheng exalted Shun, Yu, and the Duke of Zhou and cut away Gonggong and the Guan-Cai faction—hence great order whose glory endures.

sentences.zh: 孔子與季、孟偕仕於魯，李斯與叔孫俱宦於秦，定公、始皇賢季、孟、李斯而消孔子、叔孫，故以大亂，污辱至今。

translations.literal: Confucius together with the Ji and Meng served Lu; Li Si together with Shusun all held office in Qin; Duke Ding and the First Sovereign deemed the Ji, Meng, and Li Si worthy and set aside Confucius and Shusun; therefore there was great chaos, and shame stains the world to this day.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius served Lu beside the Ji and Meng; Li Si served Qin beside Shusun; Ding and the First Emperor prized Ji, Meng, and Li Si and cast off Confucius and Shusun—hence ruin whose shame lingers.

sentences.zh: 故治亂榮辱之端，在所信任；

translations.literal: Therefore the tip of order, chaos, glory, and shame lies in whom one trusts;
translations.idiomatic: So order, chaos, honor, and shame turn on whom you trust;

sentences.zh: 信任既賢，在於堅固而不移。

translations.literal: once trust is placed in the worthy, it lies in being firm and unshifting.
translations.idiomatic: once worthies win trust, hold that trust like iron.

sentences.zh: 《詩》云「我心匪石，不可轉也」。

translations.literal: The *Odes* says, “My heart is not a stone—it cannot be turned.”
translations.idiomatic: The *Odes* says, “My heart is no stone—it will not pivot.”

sentences.zh: 言守善篤也。

translations.literal: This speaks of holding fast to goodness with solidity.
translations.idiomatic: It praises steadfast good.

sentences.zh: 《易》曰「渙汗其大號」。

translations.literal: The *Changes* says, “When dispersing, sweat his great proclamation.”
translations.idiomatic: The *Changes* says, “Dispersing—sweat issues with the great command.”

sentences.zh: 言號令如汗，汗出而不反者也。

translations.literal: This means proclamations are like sweat—once sweat issues it does not return.
translations.idiomatic: Meaning edicts should run like sweat—once out, they do not crawl back.

sentences.zh: 今出善令，未能踰時而反，是反汗也；

translations.literal: Now good orders are issued, yet within less than a season they are reversed—this is reversing sweat;
translations.idiomatic: Today a good decree lasts less than a season before it is recalled—that is sweat running uphill;

sentences.zh: 用賢未能三旬而退，是轉石也。

translations.literal: employing worthies for less than thirty days and dismissing them—this is turning the stone.
translations.idiomatic: a worthy serves less than thirty days and is cast aside—that is trying to spin stone.

sentences.zh: 《論語》曰：「見不善如探湯。」

translations.literal: The *Analects* says, “Seeing the not good is like thrusting the hand into boiling water.”
translations.idiomatic: The *Analects* says, “Seeing evil should scald like boiling water.”

sentences.zh: 今二府奏佞諂不當在位，歷年而不去。

translations.literal: Now the two offices memorialize that flatterers are unfit to hold office, yet year after year they are not removed.
translations.idiomatic: The two bureaus report flatterers unfit to serve, yet year on year they stay.

sentences.zh: 故出令則如反汗，用賢則如轉石，去佞則如拔山，如此望陰陽之調，不亦難乎！

translations.literal: Therefore issuing orders is like reversing sweat, employing worthies like turning a stone, removing slanderers like uprooting a mountain—in such a state to hope for harmony of yin and yang—is it not hard!
translations.idiomatic: Orders run backward, worthies spin like stone, knaves cling like mountains—under this, hoping yin and yang will balance is vain.

sentences.zh: 是以群小窺見間隙，緣飾文字，巧言醜詆，流言飛文，譁於民間。

translations.literal: Therefore the petty band spy out gaps, dress up their writings, use crafty speech and ugly slander, floating rumor and flying documents, clamoring among the people.
translations.idiomatic: So petty men watch for cracks, polish false briefs, hiss clever slander, float rumor and pamphlets, and roil the streets.

sentences.zh: 故《詩》云：「憂心悄悄，慍于群小。」

translations.literal: Therefore the *Odes* says, “My care is anxious; I am angry at the swarm of little men.”
translations.idiomatic: The *Odes* says, “My care gnaws; I rage at the swarm of little men.”

sentences.zh: 小人成群，誠足慍也。

translations.literal: Petty men in swarms are indeed enough to anger one.
translations.idiomatic: A pack of knaves is enough to stir any honest wrath.

sentences.zh: 昔孔子與顏淵、子貢更相稱譽，不為朋黨；

translations.literal: Formerly Confucius with Yan Yuan and Zigong praised one another in turn, yet did not form a faction;
translations.idiomatic: Confucius and Yan Yuan and Zigong praised one another yet formed no faction;

sentences.zh: 禹、稷與皋陶傳相汲引，不為比周。

translations.literal: Yu and Ji with Gao Yao handed one another upward in succession, yet did not form cabals.
translations.idiomatic: Yu and Ji and Gao Yao lifted one another yet wove no cabal.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 忠於為國，無邪心也。

translations.literal: They were loyal in serving the state and had no deviant heart.
translations.idiomatic: They served the state with whole hearts and kept free of crooked will.

sentences.zh: 故賢人在上位，則引其類而聚之於朝，《易》曰「飛龍在天，大人聚也」；

translations.literal: Therefore when worthies occupy high place, they draw their kind and gather them at court—the *Changes* says, “The flying dragon is in the sky—the great man gathers.”
translations.idiomatic: Worthies aloft draw their kind to court—the *Changes* says, “Flying dragon in heaven—the great man gathers.”

sentences.zh: 在下位，則思與其類俱進，《易》曰「拔茅茹以其彙，征吉」。

translations.literal: In low place they think to advance together with their kind—the *Changes* says, “Pulling up rush by the roots, with its kind—to go is auspicious.”
translations.idiomatic: Worthies below seek to rise with their kind—the *Changes* says, “Pull rushes by the root with their clump—marching brings good.”

sentences.zh: 在上則引其類，在下則推其類，故湯用伊尹，不仁者遠，而眾賢至，類相致也。

translations.literal: Above they draw their kind, below they push their kind; therefore Tang employed Yi Yin—the unworthy withdrew and many worthies came—like calls to like.
translations.idiomatic: High or low they lift their kind; Tang used Yi Yin and the vile fled while worthies poured in—like summons like.

sentences.zh: 今佞邪與賢臣並在交戟之內，合黨共謀，違善依惡，歙歙訿訿，數設危險之言，欲以傾移主上。

translations.literal: Now flatterers and worthy ministers stand together within the crossed halberds, league in cliques to plot together, leave the good and cleave to evil, whispering and fawning, repeatedly setting forth perilous words, wishing thus to overturn and shift the sovereign.
translations.idiomatic: Now slanderers and good men stand together inside the palace halberds, league in cliques, shun right and hug wrong, whisper and smirk, and hawk peril meant to sway the throne.

sentences.zh: 如忽然用之，此天地之所以先戒，災異之所以重至者也。

translations.literal: If suddenly they are used, this is why Heaven and Earth first give warning and why portents pile up one after another.
translations.idiomatic: If such men are suddenly trusted, that is why Heaven and earth cry warning and omens stack thick.

sentences.zh: 自古明聖，未有無誅而治者也，故舜有四放之罰，而孔子有兩觀之誅，然後聖化可得而行也。

translations.literal: From antiquity clear and sage rulers have none who governed without executions; therefore Shun had the punishments of four banishments, and Confucius had the execution at the two watchers—only then could sage transformation proceed.
translations.idiomatic: No wise age ever ruled without the axe: Shun banished the four criminals; Confucius struck down the two watchers—only then could sage rule run.

sentences.zh: 今以陛下明知，誠深思天地之心，跡察兩觀之誅，覽否泰之卦，觀雨雪之詩，歷周、唐之所進以為法，原秦、魯之所消以為戒，考祥應之福，省災異之禍，以揆當世之變，放遠佞邪之黨，壞散險詖之聚，杜閉群枉之門，廣開眾正之路，決斷狐疑，分別猶豫，使是非炳然可知，則百異消滅，而眾祥並至，太平之基，萬世之利也。

translations.literal: Now with Your Majesty’s clear understanding, if you sincerely ponder Heaven and Earth’s mind, trace the execution at the two watchers, review the hexagrams of Pi and Tai, read the poems of snow and frost, take as models what Zhou and Tang promoted, take as warnings what Qin and Lu extinguished, examine the blessing of auspicious response, reflect on the harm of portents and anomalies, to measure the changes of the present age, banish far the faction of flatterers, break up gatherings of crafty peril, block the gate to many wrongs, broadly open the path for many upright men, cut off doubt, discriminate hesitation, so that right and wrong blaze plain—then the hundred prodigies will vanish and many auspices arrive together—the foundation of great peace, benefit for ten thousand generations.
translations.idiomatic: With your clear sight, ponder Heaven and earth, study the two-watchers stroke, read Obstruction and Peace, heed the snow-and-frost oaths, model Zhou and Tang, shun Qin and Lu’s fall, weigh blessing against bane, read this age’s drift, drive flatterers off, smash cabals of guile, shut the crooked gate, open the straight road, end wavering, part yes from no until truth burns plain—then prodigies die and blessings crowd in: the bedrock of peace, a boon for endless heirs.

sentences.zh: 臣幸得託肺附，誠見陰陽不調，不敢不通所聞。

translations.literal: Your servant, fortunate to be kin as lung to diaphragm, truly sees yin and yang out of tune and dares not fail to communicate what he has heard.
translations.idiomatic: I am kin close as lung to side and cannot hide what I see: yin and yang are out of tune.

sentences.zh: 竊推春秋災異，以效今事一二，條其所以，不宜宣泄。

translations.literal: Privately I extend the *Spring and Autumn*’s portents to mirror one or two of today’s affairs, listing their causes—they ought not to be spread abroad.
translations.idiomatic: I match a few Spring and Autumn omens to today’s signs and list their roots—this should not be shouted in the market.

sentences.zh: 臣謹重封昧死上。

translations.literal: Your servant respectfully re-seals and, risking death, submits.
translations.idiomatic: I seal again and, risking my life, submit.

sentences.zh: 恭、顯見其書，愈與許、史比而怨更生等。

translations.literal: When Hong and Xian saw the document, they joined Xu and Shi all the closer and resented Gengsheng and the others.
translations.idiomatic: Hong and Xian read the memorial, clung tighter to Xu and Shi, and hated Gengsheng’s party more.

sentences.zh: 堪性公方，自見孤立，遂直道而不曲。

translations.literal: Kan’s nature was just and upright; seeing himself isolated, he then held the straight road and did not bend.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Kan was square by nature; seeing himself alone, he walked straight and would not crook.

sentences.zh: 是歲夏寒，日青無光，恭、顯及許、史皆言堪、猛用事之咎。

translations.literal: That year summer was cold; the sun was greenish without light; Hong, Xian, Xu, and Shi all said it was the fault of Kan and Meng holding power.
translations.idiomatic: That summer turned cold; the sun ran pale; Hong, Xian, Xu, and Shi blamed Kan and Meng’s power for it.

sentences.zh: 上內重堪，又患眾口之寖潤，無所取信。

translations.literal: The sovereign within honored Kan, yet also worried that many mouths soaked their poison in—he had nothing by which to decide trust.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor prized Kan within yet feared the slow drip of many tongues and could not tell whom to believe.

sentences.zh: 時長安令楊興以材能幸，常稱譽堪。

translations.literal: At that time the prefect of Chang’an, Yang Xing, was favored for his talent and ability and often praised Kan.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xing, prefect of Chang’an, had won favor for his wit and often praised Kan.

sentences.zh: 上欲以為助，乃見問興：「朝臣齗齗不可光祿勳，何也？」

translations.literal: The sovereign wished to use him as aid, therefore summoned and asked Xing: “The court ministers wrangle that Kan cannot be superintendent of the palace—why?”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor meant to use him and asked, “Why do ministers snap that Kan must not be superintendent of the palace?”

sentences.zh: 興者傾巧士，謂上疑堪，因順指曰：「堪非獨不可於朝廷，自州里亦不可也。

translations.literal: Xing was a twisting, clever man; thinking the sovereign doubted Kan, he therefore followed the hint and said: “Kan is not only unfit at court—even in his home village he would not do.”
translations.idiomatic: Xing was a trimmer; guessing the emperor’s doubt, he said, “Kan is unfit not only at court—he would fail even in his home hamlet.”

sentences.zh: 臣見眾人聞堪前與劉更生等謀毀骨肉，以為當誅，故臣前言堪不可誅傷，為國養恩也。」

translations.literal: “Your servant sees that the crowd hearing how Kan earlier with Liu Gengsheng and others plotted to destroy flesh-and-blood kin thought he should be executed; therefore your servant’s earlier words that Kan must not be harmed with execution were to nurture grace for the state.”
translations.idiomatic: “The mob heard Kan once plotted with Liu Gengsheng to wound imperial kin and cried for his head; my earlier plea that he not die was to spare the state a needless stain.”

sentences.zh: 上曰：「然此何罪而誅？

translations.literal: The sovereign said, “Yet what crime is this that he should be executed?”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, “What crime is that, to merit execution?”

sentences.zh: 今宜奈何？」

translations.literal: “What ought to be done now?”
translations.idiomatic: “What should we do now?”

sentences.zh: 興曰：「臣愚以為可賜爵關內侯，食邑三百戶，勿令典事。

translations.literal: Xing said, “Your servant foolishly thinks he may be granted noble rank within the passes with three hundred households of food income, and not be ordered to handle affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Xing said, “Give him rank within the passes and three hundred households’ income, but no office.”

sentences.zh: 明主不失師傅之恩，此最策之得者也。」

translations.literal: A clear sovereign does not lose the grace due a tutor—this is the best policy to gain.”
translations.idiomatic: A wise ruler still honors a teacher—that is the cleanest course.”

sentences.zh: 上於是疑。

translations.literal: Thereupon the sovereign grew doubtful.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor hesitated.

sentences.zh: 會城門校尉諸葛豐亦言堪、猛短，上因發怒免豐。

translations.literal: It happened that the colonel of the city gates, Zhuge Feng, also spoke of Kan’s and Meng’s faults; the sovereign therefore grew angry and dismissed Feng.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuge Feng, colonel of the gates, also attacked Kan and Meng; the emperor, furious, cashiered Feng.

sentences.zh: 語在其傳。

translations.literal: The account is given in his biography.
translations.idiomatic: His story is told in his own chapter.

sentences.zh: 又曰：「豐言堪、猛貞信不立，朕閔而不治，又惜其材能未有所效，其左遷堪為河東太守，猛槐里令。」

translations.literal: He further said, “Feng said Kan and Meng did not establish steadfast good faith; We pitied them and did not punish them, yet also regretted that their talents had no place to show effect; therefore We demoted Kan to governor of Hedong and Meng to magistrate of Huaili.”
translations.idiomatic: He added, “Feng charged Kan and Meng with lacking steadfast faith; I spared them yet wasted their gifts—so I sent Kan to Hedong and Meng to Huaili.”

sentences.zh: 顯等專權日甚。

translations.literal: Xian and the others daily grew more monopolistic in power.
translations.idiomatic: Hong Gong’s faction tightened its grip by the day.

sentences.zh: 後三歲餘，孝宣廟闕災，其晦，日有蝕之。

translations.literal: More than three years later, the gate-tower of Emperor Xiaoxuan’s temple caught fire; on that month’s last day there was a solar eclipse.
translations.idiomatic: Three years on, fire struck Xuandi’s temple; on the month’s dark day the sun was eaten.

sentences.zh: 於是上召諸前言日變在堪、猛者責問，皆稽首謝。

translations.literal: Thereupon the sovereign summoned all who earlier had said the solar anomaly lay with Kan and Meng, rebuked them, and they all knocked their heads in apology.
translations.idiomatic: He called in everyone who had blamed Kan and Meng for the eclipse; they kowtowed and begged pardon.

sentences.zh: 乃因下詔曰：「河東太守堪，先帝賢之，命而傅朕。

translations.literal: He therefore issued an edict saying, “Governor of Hedong Kan— the late emperor deemed him worthy and ordered him to tutor Us.”
translations.idiomatic: Then came an edict: “Kan of Hedong—Xuandi judged him worthy and set him to instruct me.”

sentences.zh: 資質淑茂，道術通明，論議正直，秉心有常，發憤悃愊，信有憂國之心。

translations.literal: His endowment is pure and rich, his way and arts clear and bright, his discourse straight, his heart constant; he gave rein to honest bluntness and truly bore a heart anxious for the state.
translations.idiomatic: He is clear-minded, learned, outspoken, steady, blunt with honest care for the realm.”

sentences.zh: 以不能阿尊事貴，孤特寡助，抑厭遂退，卒不克明。

translations.literal: Because he could not flatter the mighty and serve the noble, he stood alone with little help, was pressed down and retired, and in the end his clarity did not prevail.
translations.idiomatic: He would not fawn on power, stood alone, was shoved aside, and his light never broke through.”

sentences.zh: 往者眾臣見異，不務自修，深惟其故，而反晻昧說天，託咎此人。

translations.literal: Formerly the host of ministers, seeing anomalies, did not labor to cultivate themselves, deeply pondered the causes, but on the contrary darkly explained Heaven and pinned blame on this man.
translations.idiomatic: When omens came, the court never looked inward but blamed Kan.”

sentences.zh: 朕不得已，出而試之，以彰其材。

translations.literal: We had no choice but to send him out and try him, to display his talents.
translations.idiomatic: I had to post him out to prove his worth.”

sentences.zh: 堪出之後，大變仍臻，眾亦嘿然。

translations.literal: After Kan went out, great changes still arrived in succession; the crowd also fell silent.
translations.idiomatic: Great prodigies kept coming even after he left—the critics fell silent.”

sentences.zh: 堪治未期年，而三老官屬有識之士詠頌其美，使者過郡，靡人不稱。

translations.literal: Kan had governed less than a full year, yet the elders and officials of discernment sang his praise; envoys passing the commandery—there was none who did not extol him.
translations.idiomatic: Inside a year the gentry of Hedong sang his praise; every envoy through the district praised him.”

sentences.zh: 此固足以彰先帝之知人，而朕有以自明也。

translations.literal: This is indeed enough to display the late emperor’s knowledge of men, and gives Us means to clarify Ourselves.
translations.idiomatic: That shows Xuandi’s eye for men—and clears my own name.”

sentences.zh: 俗人乃造端作基，非議詆欺，或引幽隱，非所宜明，意疑以類，欲以陷之，朕亦不取也。

translations.literal: Vulgar men then opened beginnings and laid foundations, criticized and slandered, or cited hidden matters not fit to clarify, suspected by analogy of kinds, wishing thereby to trap him—We also do not take that.
translations.idiomatic: Petty men now spin tales and dark hints to trap him—I will not hear it.”

sentences.zh: 朕迫于俗，不得專心，乃者天著大異，朕甚懼焉。

translations.literal: We are pressed by vulgar custom and cannot concentrate Our mind; recently Heaven has shown a great anomaly, and We are very afraid.
translations.idiomatic: Custom pins my hands; lately Heaven has shouted—I am afraid.”

sentences.zh: 今堪年衰歲暮，恐不得自信，排於異人，將安究之哉？

translations.literal: Now Kan is aged, the years declining; We fear he cannot trust himself, is pushed aside by alien men—how shall We investigate it?
translations.idiomatic: Kan is old; I fear strangers will crowd him—how can I set that right?”

sentences.zh: 其徵堪詣行在所。」

translations.literal: Summon Kan to the traveling palace.”
translations.idiomatic: Summon Kan to my traveling court.”

sentences.zh: 拜為光祿大夫，秩中二千石，領尚書事。

translations.literal: He was appointed grand counsellor of the palace at salary of fully two thousand piculs and directed the masters of writing.
translations.idiomatic: He was made grand counsellor of the palace at full two-thousand-picul rank and put over the masters of writing.

sentences.zh: 猛復為太中大夫給事中。

translations.literal: Meng was again grand counsellor of the palace serving within.
translations.idiomatic: Meng returned as grand counsellor in attendance.

sentences.zh: 顯幹尚書，尚書五人，皆其黨也。

translations.literal: Xian interfered with the masters of writing; of the five masters, all were his faction.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Xian ran the masters of writing; all five were his creatures.

sentences.zh: 堪希得見，常因顯白事，事決顯口。

translations.literal: Kan rarely could obtain audience; he always had to report affairs through Xian, and decisions came from Xian’s mouth.
translations.idiomatic: Kan could barely see the throne; every report ran through Xian’s mouth.

sentences.zh: 會堪疾瘖，不能言而卒。

translations.literal: It happened that Kan fell ill, lost his voice, could not speak, and died.
translations.idiomatic: Kan fell mute, could not speak, and died.

sentences.zh: 顯誣譖猛，令自殺於公車。

translations.literal: Xian slandered Meng and had him kill himself at the public carriage gate.
translations.idiomatic: Xian framed Meng and forced him to suicide at the carriage gate.

sentences.zh: 更生傷之，乃著疾讒、擿要、救危及世頌，凡八篇，依興古事，悼己及同類也。

translations.literal: Gengsheng grieved over this, therefore composed “Against Slander,” “Picking Essentials,” “Rescuing Peril,” and “Eulogies for the Age”—eight pieces in all—following Xing’s old cases, mourning himself and his kind.
translations.idiomatic: Gengsheng mourned them in eight essays—*Against Slander*, *Picking the Crux*, *Saving from Peril*, *Praise of the Age*—using Xing’s type, grieving for his own circle.

sentences.zh: 遂廢十餘年。

translations.literal: Thereupon he was cast aside more than ten years.
translations.idiomatic: He was left idle for a dozen years.

sentences.zh: 久之，營起昌陵，數年不成，復還歸延陵，制度泰奢。

translations.literal: After a long time, when they had raised Changling and for several years it was not completed, they returned again to Yanling; the scale was excessively lavish.
translations.idiomatic: Years passed: builders raised Changling, could not finish, fell back to Yanling, and spent wildly.

sentences.zh: 向上疏諫曰：

translations.literal: Xiang submitted a memorial remonstrating, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xiang then memorialized:

sentences.zh: 臣聞易曰：「安不忘危，存不忘亡，是以身安而國家可保也。」

translations.literal: Your servant has heard the *Changes* says, “In peace do not forget peril, in survival do not forget destruction—thereby body is at peace and state and family may be preserved.”
translations.idiomatic: The *Changes* says, “Do not forget danger in safety nor ruin in survival—then person and realm endure.”

sentences.zh: 故賢聖之君，博觀終始，窮極事情，而是非分明。

translations.literal: Therefore sage and worthy rulers broadly observe beginning and end, exhaust the facts of affairs, and right and wrong are distinct.
translations.idiomatic: Wise kings scan the whole arc of events until truth sorts itself.

sentences.zh: 王者必通三統，明天命所授者博，非獨一姓也。

translations.literal: A true king must comprehend the three successions, clarifying that Heaven’s mandate is broad in conferral and not confined to one surname.
translations.idiomatic: A king must grasp the three successions: Heaven’s mandate is wide, not locked to one clan.

sentences.zh: 孔子論詩，至於「殷士膚敏，祼將于京」，喟然歎曰：「大哉天命！

translations.literal: When Confucius discussed the *Odes* down to “The Yin knights were alert and keen, performing the libation rite in the capital,” he sighed and said, “How great Heaven’s mandate!
translations.idiomatic: When Confucius read the *Odes* to “Yin knights, keen in the capital rite,” he sighed, “Great is Heaven’s command!

sentences.zh: 善不可不傳于子孫，是以富貴無常；

translations.literal: Goodness must be passed to descendants—therefore wealth and honor are not constant;
translations.idiomatic: Virtue must reach sons and grandsons—so fortune is never fixed;

sentences.zh: 不如是，則王公其何以戒慎，民萌何以勸勉？」

translations.literal: if it were not so, how would kings and dukes take warning, how would the common folk be encouraged?”
translations.idiomatic: otherwise how would lords learn fear and commoners learn zeal?”

sentences.zh: 蓋傷微子之事周，而痛殷之亡也。

translations.literal: This was grieving the viscount of Wei’s serving Zhou and lamenting Yin’s fall.
translations.idiomatic: He mourned Weizi’s turn to Zhou and Yin’s end.

sentences.zh: 雖有堯舜之聖，不能化丹朱之子；

translations.literal: Though there be Yao and Shun’s sagehood, they could not transform the sons of Dan Zhu;
translations.idiomatic: Yao and Shun could not mend Dan Zhu’s sons;

sentences.zh: 雖有禹湯之德，不能訓末孫之桀紂。

translations.literal: though there be Yu and Tang’s virtue, they could not instruct the last descendants Jie and Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: Yu and Tang could not school Jie and Zhou’s last seed.

sentences.zh: 自古及今，未有不亡之國也。

translations.literal: From antiquity to the present there has never been a state that did not perish.
translations.idiomatic: No realm under heaven has lived forever.

sentences.zh: 昔高皇帝既滅秦，將都雒陽，感寤劉敬之言，自以德不及周，而賢於秦，遂徙都關中，依周之德，因秦之阻。

translations.literal: Formerly the August Emperor Gao, having destroyed Qin, was about to make his capital at Luoyang; awakened by Liu Jing’s words, he considered his virtue not equal to Zhou’s yet better than Qin’s, therefore moved the capital into the passes, relying on Zhou’s virtue and using Qin’s natural barriers.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu meant to settle at Luoyang until Liu Jing woke him; judging himself below Zhou yet above Qin, he moved within the passes, borrowing Zhou’s virtue and Qin’s terrain.

sentences.zh: 世之長短，以德為效，故常戰栗，不敢諱亡。

translations.literal: The length of a line’s fortune is measured by its virtue as proof; therefore he always trembled with fear and did not dare hide from the word “perish.”
translations.idiomatic: A dynasty’s span rides on virtue—so he trembled and never dodged the word “fall.”

sentences.zh: 孔子所謂「富貴無常」，蓋謂此也。

translations.literal: What Confucius called “wealth and honor are not constant” speaks to this.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius’ “riches shift” means just this.

sentences.zh: 孝文皇帝居霸陵，北臨廁，意悽愴悲懷，顧謂群臣曰：「嗟乎！

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaowen dwelt at Baling, looking north toward the slope; his thoughts were mournful and grieved; turning to his ministers he said, “Alas!
translations.idiomatic: Wendi walked Baling’s ridge, gazed north, grew sad, and told his court, “Ah!

sentences.zh: 以北山石為槨，用紵絮斮陳漆其間，豈可動哉！」

translations.literal: if we take north-mountain stone for outer coffin, pack ramie floss chopped fine and lacquer the joints, could it be moved!”
translations.idiomatic: North-shoulder stone for a shell, ramie floss chopped fine and lacquered in the seams—who could breach that!”

sentences.zh: 張釋之進曰：「使其中有可欲，雖錮南山猶有隙；

translations.literal: Zhang Shizhi advanced and said, “If within there is something desirable, though you bolt South Mountain there would still be a crack;
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Shizhi answered, “If greed hides inside, even sealing Mount Zhongnan leaves a seam;

sentences.zh: 使其中無可欲，雖無石槨，又何慼焉？」

translations.literal: if within there is nothing desirable, though there be no stone coffin, what is there to worry?”
translations.idiomatic: if nothing tempts the thief, even without stone, why fear?”

sentences.zh: 夫死者無終極，而國家有廢興，故釋之之言，為無窮計也。

translations.literal: The dead have no end, yet states have rise and fall; therefore Shizhi’s words planned for the endless.
translations.idiomatic: Death does not end, but dynasties do—Shizhi spoke for eternity.”

sentences.zh: 孝文寤焉，遂薄葬，不起山墳。

translations.literal: Xiaowen awoke to this, therefore practiced thin burial and did not raise a mountain tumulus.
translations.idiomatic: Wendi took the point, buried plainly, raised no mound.

sentences.zh: 《易》曰：「古之葬者，厚衣之以薪，臧之中野，不封不樹。

translations.literal: The *Changes* says, “In antiquity those who buried thickly clothed the dead in firewood, hid them in the middle of the wilds, raised no mound and planted no tree;
translations.idiomatic: The *Changes* says, “The ancients wrapped the dead in brush, laid them in the plain, heaped no mound, planted no tree;

sentences.zh: 後世聖人易之以棺槨。」

translations.literal: later sages replaced this with inner and outer coffins.”
translations.idiomatic: later sages gave them inner and outer shells.”

sentences.zh: 棺槨之作，自黃帝始。

translations.literal: The making of inner and outer coffins began with the Yellow Emperor.
translations.idiomatic: Coffins began with the Yellow Emperor.

sentences.zh: 黃帝葬於橋山，堯葬濟陰，丘壟皆小，葬具甚微。

translations.literal: The Yellow Emperor was buried at Mount Qiao; Yao was buried in Jiyin; their mounds were all small, the burial goods very slight.
translations.idiomatic: The Yellow Emperor lies at Qiao; Yao at Jiyin—low mounds, scant gear.

sentences.zh: 舜葬蒼梧，二妃不從。

translations.literal: Shun was buried at Cangwu; the two consorts did not accompany him.
translations.idiomatic: Shun sleeps at Cangwu without his two wives at his side.

sentences.zh: 禹葬會稽，不改其列。

translations.literal: Yu was buried at Kuaiji, not altering the local rows.
translations.idiomatic: Yu rests at Kuaiji, leaving the old rows untouched.

sentences.zh: 殷湯無葬處。

translations.literal: Tang of Yin had no burial place.
translations.idiomatic: Shang Tang has no known tomb.

sentences.zh: 文、武、周公葬於畢，秦穆公葬於雍橐泉宮祈年館下，樗里子葬於武庫，皆無丘隴之處。

translations.literal: King Wen, King Wu, and the Duke of Zhou were buried at Bi; Duke Mu of Qin was buried below the prayer-hall of the Qinuoquan Palace at Yong; Juzi was buried at the armory—all without mound sites.
translations.idiomatic: Wen, Wu, and the Duke of Zhou lie at Bi; Duke Mu of Qin under Yong’s Qinuoquan prayer hall; Juzi at the arsenal—none under great heaps.

sentences.zh: 此聖帝明王賢君智士遠覽獨慮無窮之計也。

translations.literal: These are plans of far sight and lone reflection by sage emperors, bright kings, worthy rulers, and wise men, gazing to the endless.
translations.idiomatic: That is how sage kings plan for the long view.
