sentences.zh: 其賢臣孝子亦承命順意而薄葬之，此誠奉安君父，忠孝之至也。

translations.literal: Their worthy ministers and filial sons likewise received orders, followed intent, and buried them thinly—this is indeed truly to settle lord and father in peace, the utmost of loyalty and filial piety.
translations.idiomatic: Good sons and loyal ministers obeyed and buried lightly—true peace for father and lord, the crown of duty.

sentences.zh: 逮至吳王闔閭，違禮厚葬，十有餘年，越人發之。

translations.literal: Down to King Helü of Wu, who violated ritual with a thick burial—after more than ten years the men of Yue dug him up.
translations.idiomatic: Helü of Wu broke rites for a rich tomb; Yue opened it within a decade.

sentences.zh: 及秦惠文、武、昭、嚴襄五王，皆大作丘隴，多其瘞臧，咸盡發掘暴露，甚足悲也。

translations.literal: And the five kings of Qin—Huiwen, Wu, Zhao, Yanxiang, and Xiang—all raised great mounds and piled rich grave goods; all were fully dug up and exposed—very pitiable.
translations.idiomatic: Qin’s Huiwen, Wu, Zhao, Yanxiang, and Xiangwang piled high tombs and treasure; robbers stripped every one—a bitter sight.

sentences.zh: 秦始皇帝葬於驪山之阿，下錮三泉，上崇山墳，其高五十餘丈，周回五里有餘；

translations.literal: The First Emperor of Qin was buried at the foot of Mount Li, bolted down to the three springs below, raised a mountain tumulus above more than fifty ren in height, over five li in circuit;
translations.idiomatic: The First Emperor slept at Li’s foot, clamped to the triple spring, crowned by a mound fifty ren high and five li around;

sentences.zh: 石槨為游館，人膏為燈燭，水銀為江海，黃金為鳧雁。

translations.literal: stone outer coffin made a touring lodge, human fat for lamps, quicksilver for rivers and seas, gold for ducks and geese.
translations.idiomatic: stone chambers became halls, human tallow fed lamps, mercury stood for seas, gold for wildfowl.

sentences.zh: 珍寶之臧，機械之變，棺槨之麗，宮館之盛，不可勝原。

translations.literal: The hoard of treasures, the devices of craft, the splendor of coffins, the magnificence of palace lodges—cannot all be traced.
translations.idiomatic: Gems, traps, painted coffins, palace rooms—no inventory can hold them.

sentences.zh: 又多殺宮人，生薶工匠，計以萬數。

translations.literal: He further killed many palace people and buried artisans alive—reckoned in myriads.
translations.idiomatic: He slaughtered concubines and buried craftsmen alive—counted in tens of thousands.

sentences.zh: 天下苦其役而反之，驪山之作未成，而周章百萬之師至其下矣。

translations.literal: All under heaven suffered his labor and turned against him; the Mount Li work was not finished when Zhou Zhang’s host of a million reached its foot.
translations.idiomatic: The realm groaned under his corvée; Li was not done when Zhou Zhang’s million men stood below it.

sentences.zh: 項籍燔其宮室營宇，往者咸見發掘。

translations.literal: Xiang Ji burned his palaces and camps; later men all saw them dug up.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu torched the halls; later ages dug the wreck.

sentences.zh: 其後牧兒亡羊，羊入其鑿，牧者持火照求羊，失火燒其臧槨。

translations.literal: Afterward a shepherd boy lost a sheep; the sheep entered its bore; the shepherd took fire to seek the sheep, lost fire, and burned the hidden coffin.
translations.idiomatic: A herder’s sheep fell into a shaft; he lowered a torch to find it and set the hidden vault ablaze.

sentences.zh: 自古至今，葬未有盛如始皇者也，數年之間，外被項籍之災，內離牧豎之禍，豈不哀哉！

translations.literal: From antiquity to the present, burial has never been more splendid than the First Emperor’s; within a few years, without suffered Xiang Ji’s disaster from without and the shepherd boys’ calamity from within—is it not pitiable!
translations.idiomatic: No tomb ever matched his; within years it met Xiang Yu without and a boy’s torch within—what grief!

sentences.zh: 是故德彌厚者葬彌薄，知愈深者葬愈微。

translations.literal: Therefore the thicker the virtue the thinner the burial; the deeper the knowledge the slighter the burial.
translations.idiomatic: Deep virtue means spare tomb; deep sense means modest grave.

sentences.zh: 無德寡知，其葬愈厚，丘隴彌高，宮廟甚麗，發掘必速。

translations.literal: Without virtue and with shallow knowledge, the thicker the burial, the higher the mound, the more splendid the temples—the quicker the digging open.
translations.idiomatic: The hollower the man, the grander the pile—the faster the looters come.

sentences.zh: 由是觀之，明暗之效，葬之吉凶，昭然可見矣。

translations.literal: Viewed from this, the effect of brightness and darkness, the good and ill augury of burial, blaze plain and may be seen.
translations.idiomatic: So light and dark, lucky and ill tombs, stare you in the face.

sentences.zh: 周德既衰而奢侈，宣王賢而中興，更為儉宮室，小寢廟。

translations.literal: Zhou’s virtue had declined and turned extravagant; King Xuan was worthy and revived the house, again making palaces frugal and shrines and bedchambers small.
translations.idiomatic: Late Zhou grew wasteful; worthy King Xuan shrank halls and shrines.

sentences.zh: 詩人美之，斯干之詩是也，上章道宮室之如制，下章言子孫之眾多也。

translations.literal: The poets praised this—the poem “Spreading Dry” is such; the upper stanza speaks of palace chambers according to measure, the lower stanza speaks of the multitude of descendants.
translations.idiomatic: The *Odes* praise it in “Spreading Dry”—upper lines on lawful halls, lower on many heirs.

sentences.zh: 及魯嚴公刻飾宗廟，多築臺囿，後嗣再絕，春秋刺焉。

translations.literal: When Duke Yan of Lu carved and adorned the ancestral temple and built many terraces and parks, his posterity twice died out—the *Spring and Autumn* stabbed him.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Yan of Lu gilded the temple and piled terraces until his line failed twice—the *Spring and Autumn* flays him.

sentences.zh: 周宣如彼而昌，魯、秦如此而絕，是則奢儉之得失也。

translations.literal: King Xuan of Zhou was thus and flourished; Lu and Qin were thus and cut off—this is the gain and loss of extravagance and frugality.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou rose on thrift; Lu and Qin fell on waste—that is the sum.

sentences.zh: 書奏，上甚感向言，而不能從其計。

translations.literal: When the memorial was submitted, the sovereign was deeply moved by Xiang’s words but could not follow his plan.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor felt Xiang’s words yet could not follow them.

sentences.zh: 向睹俗彌奢淫，而趙、衛之屬起微賤，踰禮制。

translations.literal: Xiang saw custom grow ever more extravagant and dissolute, while Zhao, Wei, and their like rose from the mean and overstepped ritual norms.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang watched fashion turn wild while Zhao, Wei, and such climbed from low birth past every rule.

sentences.zh: 向以為王教由內及外，自近者始。

translations.literal: Xiang thought kingly teaching proceeds from inside to outside, beginning with what is near.
translations.idiomatic: He held that royal teaching starts within and spreads outward, from the near first.

sentences.zh: 故採取詩書所載賢妃貞婦，興國顯家可法則，及孽嬖亂亡者，序次為列女傳，凡八篇，以戒天子。

translations.literal: Therefore he collected what the *Odes* and *Documents* record of worthy consorts and chaste wives who raised states and made families illustrious and may be taken as models, together with favored concubines who brought chaos and ruin, arranged them in order as “Arrayed Biographies of Women”—eight pieces in all—to warn the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: So he culled the classics for good wives and bad favorites, set them in *Biographies of Exemplary Women* in eight scrolls to warn the throne.

sentences.zh: 及采傳記行事，著新序、說苑凡五十篇奏之。

translations.literal: Further he culled deeds from transmitted records and composed *New Arrangements* and *Garden of Persuasions*—fifty pieces in all—and presented them.
translations.idiomatic: He also compiled tales into *New Prefaces* and *Garden of Persuasions*, fifty scrolls, and presented them.

sentences.zh: 數上疏言得失，陳法戒。

translations.literal: Repeatedly he submitted memorials speaking of gain and loss, setting forth models and warnings.
translations.idiomatic: He kept memorializing on right and wrong, law and caution.

sentences.zh: 書數十上，以助觀覽，補遺闕。

translations.literal: Several tens of documents went up to aid the sovereign’s inspection and supply what was missing.
translations.idiomatic: Dozens of memorials went up to fill gaps in the ruler’s reading.

sentences.zh: 上雖不能盡用，然內嘉其言，常嗟歎之。

translations.literal: Although the sovereign could not use them all, within he praised his words and often sighed in admiration.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor could not act on all of it but prized the counsel and often sighed praise.

sentences.zh: 時上無繼嗣，政由王氏出，災異尤甚。

translations.literal: At that time the sovereign had no successor; government issued from the Wang clan; portents grew thick indeed.
translations.idiomatic: The throne lacked an heir; the Wangs ran the state; omens thickened.

sentences.zh: 向雅奇陳湯智謀，與相親友，獨謂湯曰：「災異如此，而外家日甚，其漸必危劉氏。

translations.literal: Xiang always thought Chen Tang’s stratagems remarkable and was close friends with him; alone he said to Tang, “Portents are thus, while the maternal kin grow daily stronger—the trend will surely endanger the Liu house.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang admired Chen Tang’s wit and was his friend; alone he told him, “Omens pile up while affines wax—the Liu house is sliding toward peril.

sentences.zh: 吾幸得同姓末屬，絫世蒙漢厚恩，身為宗室遺老，歷事三主。

translations.literal: Your servant, fortunate to share the surname as a distant twig, has for generations received Han’s deep favor; personally, as an old survivor of the imperial clan, I have served three lords.
translations.idiomatic: I am a twig of the Liu line, long fed by Han, an old clan man who has served three emperors.

sentences.zh: 上以我先帝舊臣，每進見常加優禮，吾而不言，孰當言者？」

translations.literal: The sovereign, because I am a former minister of the late emperor, whenever I advance to audience always adds excellent courtesy—if I do not speak, who should speak?”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor still honors me as Xuandi’s old servant—if I stay silent, who will speak?”

sentences.zh: 向遂上封事極諫曰：

translations.literal: Xiang thereupon submitted a sealed memorial remonstrating to the utmost, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Xiang then filed a sealed remonstrance:

sentences.zh: 臣聞人君莫不欲安，然而常危，莫不欲存，然而常亡，失御臣之術也。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that no lord of men does not wish to be secure, yet he is often in peril; none does not wish to survive, yet he often perishes—because he has lost the art of controlling ministers.
translations.idiomatic: No ruler wants danger yet courts it, wants ruin yet finds it—he has forgotten how to master his ministers.

sentences.zh: 夫大臣操權柄，持國政，未有不為害者也。

translations.literal: Great ministers who grasp power handles and hold state authority—there has never been one who did not become harmful.
translations.idiomatic: Ministers who clutch the levers never fail to harm the throne.

sentences.zh: 昔晉有六卿，齊有田、崔，衛有孫、甯，魯有季、孟，常掌國事，世執朝柄。

translations.literal: Formerly Jin had the six ministers; Qi had Tian and Cui; Wei had Sun and Ning; Lu had Ji and Meng—they constantly held state affairs and for generations grasped the court’s handle.
translations.idiomatic: Jin had six clans, Qi Tian and Cui, Wei Sun and Ning, Lu Ji and Meng—each ran the state for generations.

sentences.zh: 終後田氏取齊；

translations.literal: In the end afterward the Tian house took Qi;
translations.idiomatic: At last Tian seized Qi;

sentences.zh: 六卿分晉；

translations.literal: the six ministers divided Jin;
translations.idiomatic: six houses carved Jin;

sentences.zh: 崔杼弒其君光；

translations.literal: Cui Zhu murdered his lord Guang;
translations.idiomatic: Cui Zhu killed Duke Guang;

sentences.zh: 孫林父、甯殖出其君衎，弒其君剽；

translations.literal: Sun Linfu and Ning Zhi expelled their lord Xian and murdered their lord Shang;
translations.idiomatic: Sun and Ning drove Duke Xian of Wei into exile and assassinated Duke Shang;

sentences.zh: 季氏八佾舞於庭，三家者以雍徹，並專國政，卒逐昭公。

translations.literal: the Ji clan performed the eight rows of dancers in its courtyard; the three houses used the “Yong” hymn at the removal of the sacrificial vessels—together they monopolized state power and in the end drove out Duke Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Ji danced eight rows in his yard; three houses sang “Yong” at the sacrifice—then seized power and chased Duke Zhao.

sentences.zh: 周大夫尹氏筦朝事，濁亂王室，子朝、子猛更立，連年乃定。

translations.literal: The Zhou grandee Yin Shi managed court affairs, befouled and disordered the royal house; Zichao and Zimeng were enthroned in alternation; only after successive years was it settled.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou’s Yin Shi ran the court, fouled the royal house, set Zichao and Zimeng by turns, and years passed before order returned.

sentences.zh: 故經曰「王室亂」，又曰「尹氏殺王子克」，甚之也。

translations.literal: Therefore the classic says “the royal house is in disorder,” and again says “the Yin clan killed the prince Ke”—very severe.
translations.idiomatic: Hence the classic cries “the royal house falls in chaos” and “Yin killed prince Ke”—the blame is heavy.”

sentences.zh: 春秋舉成敗，錄禍福，如此類甚眾，皆陰盛而陽微，下失臣道之所致也。

translations.literal: The *Spring and Autumn* raises success and failure, records calamity and blessing; cases of this sort are very many—all come from yin growing strong while yang grows faint, from below losing the way of serving ministers.
translations.idiomatic: The classic piles examples of rise and fall—almost all trace yin swelling while yang thins, ministers forgetting their station.

sentences.zh: 故書曰：「臣之有作威作福，害于而家，凶于而國。」

translations.literal: Therefore the *Documents* says, “When ministers make might and make blessing, it harms your house and is ominous for your state.”
translations.idiomatic: The *Documents* warns: “When ministers seize power and favor, your house suffers and the state totters.”

sentences.zh: 孔子曰「祿去公室，政逮大夫」，危亡之兆。

translations.literal: Confucius said, “When salary leaves the ducal hall, government reaches the grandees”—a sign of peril and ruin.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, “When pay leaves the public hall for private grandees,” ruin knocks.

sentences.zh: 秦昭王舅穰侯及涇陽、葉陽君專國擅勢，上假太后之威，三人者權重於昭王，家富於秦國，國甚危殆，賴寤范睢之言，而秦復存。

translations.literal: King Zhaowang of Qin’s maternal uncles, Marquis Rang of Wei and the lords of Jingyang and Yeyang, monopolized the state and arrogated power; above they borrowed the empress dowager’s awesomeness—the three men’s weight exceeded King Zhao’s, their houses were richer than Qin’s state; the state was in extreme peril, but thanks to waking to Fan Ju’s words Qin survived again.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao of Qin let uncles Rang, Jingyang, and Yeyang run the realm; they outweighed the king until Fan Ju’s wake-up call saved Qin.

sentences.zh: 二世委任趙高，專權自恣，壅蔽大臣，終有閻樂望夷之禍，秦遂以亡。

translations.literal: The Second Emperor entrusted affairs to Zhao Gao, who monopolized power and indulged himself, blocked and blinded the great ministers, and in the end there was the disaster of Yan Le at Wangyi—Qin thereupon perished.
translations.idiomatic: Ershi handed the realm to Zhao Gao until Yan Le struck at Wangyi and Qin fell.

sentences.zh: 近事不遠，即漢所代也。

translations.literal: Recent events are not distant—they are what Han replaced.
translations.idiomatic: That was yesterday—the dynasty your Han replaced.

sentences.zh: 漢興，諸呂無道，擅相尊王。

translations.literal: When Han arose, the Lüs were without the Way and presumptuously set one another up as kings.
translations.idiomatic: At Han’s rise the Lü clan broke the Way and crowned each other.

sentences.zh: 呂產、呂祿席太后之寵，據將相之位，兼南北軍之眾，擁梁、趙王之尊，驕盈無厭，欲危劉氏。

translations.literal: Lü Chan and Lü Lu, riding the empress dowager’s favor, occupied general and minister posts, held the hosts of south and north armies, embraced the honor of kings of Liang and Zhao, swelled with pride insatiably, and wished to endanger the Liu house.
translations.idiomatic: Lü Chan and Lü Lu leaned on the empress dowager, held sword and tally, both armies, and the kings of Liang and Zhao, and meant to unseat Liu.

sentences.zh: 賴忠正大臣絳侯、朱虛侯等竭誠盡節以誅滅之，然後劉氏復安。

translations.literal: Thanks to loyal and upright great ministers—the Marquis of Jiang and the Marquis of Zhuxu and others—who exhausted sincerity and fulfilled integrity to execute and destroy them, afterward the Liu house was again secure.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Bo of Jiang and Liu Zhang of Zhuxu and their party slew them and restored the Liu.

sentences.zh: 今王氏一姓乘朱輪華轂者二十三人，青紫貂蟬充盈幄內，魚鱗左右。

translations.literal: Now the Wang surname alone has twenty-three men riding vermilion wheels and ornamented hubs; blue and purple, sable and cicada fill the inner curtains and flank him like fish scales.
translations.idiomatic: Twenty-three Wangs ride grand chariots; purple sable fills the curtains like scales.

sentences.zh: 大將軍秉事用權，五侯驕奢僭盛，並作威福，擊斷自恣，行汙而寄治，身私而託公，依東宮之尊，假甥舅之親，以為威重。

translations.literal: The general-in-chief wields affairs and uses power; the five marquises are arrogant, extravagant, and presumptuous beyond measure; together they make might and blessing, strike decisions and indulge their will, conduct filthy deeds while posing as good order, pursue private ends while borrowing public name; relying on the heir’s palace’s honor and borrowing nephew-uncle closeness, they build formidable weight.
translations.idiomatic: The general-in-chief rules; five marquises strut; they play judge while filthy, steal in the name of order, lean on the heir-apparent’s hall and affinal ties to loom large.

sentences.zh: 尚書九卿州牧郡守皆出其門，筦執樞機，朋黨比周。

translations.literal: Masters of writing, nine ministers, regional shepherds, and grand administrators all issue from their gate; they grasp the pivot and axle, form factions and cabals.
translations.idiomatic: Secretaries, nine ministers, governors—all are their clients; they hold the hinge and pack court.

sentences.zh: 稱譽者登進，忤恨者誅傷；

translations.literal: Those who praise them rise; those who offend them are struck down;
translations.idiomatic: Flatterers rise; dissenters bleed;

sentences.zh: 游談者助之說，執政者為之言。

translations.literal: wandering talkers assist their persuasions; those in power speak for them.
translations.idiomatic: gossips polish their tale; ministers echo it.

sentences.zh: 排擯宗室，孤弱公族，其有智能者，尤非毀而不進。

translations.literal: They push aside the imperial clan, isolate and weaken the ducal house; where there is intelligence they especially slander and block advance.
translations.idiomatic: They shove Liu kin aside, starve the house; sharp men they slander hardest.

sentences.zh: 遠絕宗室之任，不令得給事朝省，恐其與己分權；

translations.literal: They cut off the imperial clan from office, not allowing them to serve in court audiences, fearing they would share power;
translations.idiomatic: They bar clan men from the hall lest they split authority;

sentences.zh: 數稱燕王、蓋主以疑上心，避諱呂、霍而弗肯稱。

translations.literal: they repeatedly cite the king of Yan and the princess of Gai to make the sovereign’s mind doubtful, yet avoid naming the Lüs and Huo.
translations.idiomatic: they harp on Yan and Princess Gai to cloud your mind yet never say “Lü” or “Huo.”

sentences.zh: 內有管、蔡之萌，外假周公之論，兄弟據重，宗族磐互。

translations.literal: Within there is the sprout of Guan and Cai; without they borrow the Duke of Zhou’s rhetoric; brothers hold heavy posts; the lineage is interlocked like a boulder.
translations.idiomatic: Inside it is Guan and Cai again; outside they wrap in Duke of Zhou’s cloth—brothers heavy, clan interlocked.

sentences.zh: 歷上古至秦漢，外戚僭貴未有如王氏者也。

translations.literal: From high antiquity down to Qin and Han, no affinal kin have overstepped honor like the Wangs.
translations.idiomatic: No affines in history ever climbed like the Wangs.

sentences.zh: 雖周皇甫、秦穰侯、漢武安、呂、霍、上官之屬，皆不及也。

translations.literal: Even Zhou’s Huangfu, Qin’s Marquis Rang, Han’s Marquis of Wu’an, the Lüs, Huo, and Shangguan—these do not match them.
translations.idiomatic: Huangfu, Rang, Wu’an, Lü, Huo, Shangguan—all pale beside them.

sentences.zh: 物盛必有非常之變先見，為其人微象。

translations.literal: When a thing flourishes there must first appear extraordinary changes as faint images for its person.
translations.idiomatic: Great power always throws omens first—small signs before the storm.

sentences.zh: 孝昭帝時，冠石立於泰山，仆柳起於上林。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Xiaozhao, a capped stone stood at Mount Tai, and a fallen willow rose in Shanglin.
translations.idiomatic: Zhaodi’s age: a crowned stone on Tai, a dead willow standing in Shanglin.

sentences.zh: 而孝宣帝即位，今王氏先祖墳墓在濟南者，其梓柱生枝葉，扶疏上出屋，根艺地中，雖立石起柳，無以過此之明也。

translations.literal: When Emperor Xiaoxuan took the throne, the catalpa pillars on the Wangs’ ancestral tombs at Jinan sprouted branches and leaves, spreading upward beyond the roof, roots reaching into the ground—though standing stone and rising willow occurred, nothing surpasses this in clarity.
translations.idiomatic: Xuandi’s rise saw Wang tombs at Jinan sprout roof-high branches from the coffin posts—clearer omen than stone or willow.

sentences.zh: 事勢不兩大，王氏與劉氏亦且不並立，如下有泰山之安，則上有累卵之危。

translations.literal: Two powers cannot both be great; Wang and Liu likewise cannot stand together; if below there is Mount Tai’s security, then above there is the peril of eggs piled high.
translations.idiomatic: Two suns cannot shine; Wang and Liu cannot share the sky—if they stand firm as Tai, you teeter like stacked eggs.

sentences.zh: 陛下為人子孫，守持宗廟，而令國祚移於外親，降為皁隸，縱不為身，奈宗廟何！

translations.literal: Your Majesty, as descendant and child, guards the ancestral temples, yet would let the state’s fate pass to affinal kin and sink to black slaves—even if you do not do it for yourself, what of the ancestral temples!
translations.idiomatic: You hold the Liu shrines yet would hand the tally to affines and become a bondsman—think of the altars if not yourself!

sentences.zh: 婦人內夫家，外父母家，此亦非皇太后之福也。

translations.literal: A woman is inward to her husband’s house, outward to her parents’ house—this is also not a blessing to the grand empress dowager.
translations.idiomatic: A wife belongs to her husband’s house, not her father’s—this cannot bless the empress dowager either.

sentences.zh: 孝宣皇帝不與舅平昌、樂昌侯權，所以安全之也。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaoxuan did not give power to his uncles the marquises of Pingchang and Lechang—thereby he kept them secure.
translations.idiomatic: Xuandi starved his uncles of Pingchang and Lechang of power—and kept them alive.

sentences.zh: 夫明者起福於無形，銷患於未然。

translations.literal: The clear-sighted raise blessing where it has no shape and melt disaster before it exists.
translations.idiomatic: Wise men plant fortune unseen and kill trouble before it buds.

sentences.zh: 宜發明詔，吐德音，援近宗室，親而納信，黜遠外戚，毋授以政，皆罷令就弟，以則效先帝之所行，厚安外戚，全其宗族，誠東宮之意，外家之福也。

translations.literal: It is fitting to issue a brilliant edict, utter virtuous sound, draw near the imperial clan, treating them as close and admitting trust, push far the affinal kin, not handing them government, all dismissed to their mansions to imitate what the former emperors practiced, generously securing the affinal kin and preserving their lineage—this is truly the heir’s palace’s intent and the affinal houses’ blessing.
translations.idiomatic: Issue a clear edict: embrace Liu kin, starve Wang office, send affines home as old emperors did—blessing to East Palace and to the Wangs themselves.

sentences.zh: 王氏永存，保其爵祿，劉氏長安，不失社稷，所以褒睦外內之姓，子子孫孫無疆之計也。

translations.literal: Let the Wangs forever survive, preserving their noble rank and salary; let the Liu house long be secure, not losing altars and soil—this is how to honor and harmonize inner and outer surnames, a plan for sons and grandsons without limit.
translations.idiomatic: Wangs keep rank, Lius keep the realm—harmony inside and out for endless heirs.

sentences.zh: 如不行此策，田氏復見於今，六卿必起於漢，為後嗣憂，昭昭甚明，不可不深圖，不可不蚤慮。

translations.literal: If this policy is not followed, Tian’s house will appear again today, the six ministers will surely arise in Han—sorrow for later heirs is blazing plain; it cannot but be deeply planned, cannot but be early considered.
translations.idiomatic: Ignore this and Tian seizes Qi again, six ministers split Jin in Han—your heirs will curse you; plan now, plan early.

sentences.zh: 《易》曰：「君不密，則失臣；

translations.literal: The *Changes* says, “If the lord is not secret, he loses his ministers;
translations.idiomatic: The *Changes*: “The ruler who leaks loses ministers;

sentences.zh: 臣不密，則失身；

translations.literal: if ministers are not secret, they lose their persons;
translations.idiomatic: the minister who leaks loses his skin;

sentences.zh: 幾事不密，則害成。」

translations.literal: if matters of moment are not secret, harm is completed.”
translations.idiomatic: the plot that leaks fails.”

sentences.zh: 唯陛下深留聖思，審固幾密，覽往事之戒，以折中取信，居萬安之實，用保宗廟，久承皇太后，天下幸甚。

translations.literal: Only may Your Majesty deeply retain sagely thought, examine and firm secrecy on matters of moment, review the warnings of past affairs, take the balanced mean to win trust, dwell in the reality of ten-thousandfold security, thereby to guard the ancestral temples, long to receive the grand empress dowager—under heaven will be greatly fortunate.
translations.idiomatic: Seal your counsel, read old omens, choose the middle path, sit in true safety, guard the shrines, serve the dowager long—Heaven will smile.

sentences.zh: 書奏，天子召見向，歎息悲傷其意，謂曰：「君且休矣，吾將思之。」

translations.literal: When the memorial was submitted, the Son of Heaven summoned Xiang to audience, sighed and grieved at his meaning, and said to him, “For now, sir, rest; We shall think on it.”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor called him in, wept at his words, and said, “Rest; I will think.”

sentences.zh: 以向為中壘校尉。

translations.literal: He appointed Xiang colonel of the garrisoned encampment.
translations.idiomatic: He made Xiang colonel of the garrisoned encampment.

sentences.zh: 向為人簡易無威儀，廉靖樂道，不交接世俗，專積思於經術，晝誦書傳，夜觀星宿，或不寐達旦。

translations.literal: Xiang as a person was simple and easy, without awe-inspiring deportment; honest and quiet, he delighted in the Way, did not mingle with the vulgar world, but concentrated his thought on the classics, by day chanting documents and transmissions, by night observing the asterisms, sometimes not sleeping until dawn.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang was plain, unshowy, clean, loved learning, shunned society, read by day, watched stars by night, often till dawn.

sentences.zh: 元延中，星孛東井，蜀郡岷山崩雍江。

translations.literal: In the Yuanyan period a broom-star blazed at Eastern Well; in Shu commandery the Min mountains collapsed and dammed the river.
translations.idiomatic: Yuanyan: a comet in Eastern Well; Minshan fell in Shu and choked the river.

sentences.zh: 向惡此異，語在五行志。

translations.literal: Xiang hated this anomaly; the account is in the “Treatise on the Five Phases.”
translations.idiomatic: He dreaded that sign—see the Five Phases treatise.

sentences.zh: 懷不能已，復上奏，其辭曰：

translations.literal: Unable to contain himself, he again submitted a memorial; its words said:
translations.idiomatic: He could not rest and memorialized again:

sentences.zh: 臣聞帝舜戒伯禹，毋若丹朱敖；

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that Emperor Shun warned Yu, “Do not be like Dan Zhu arrogant”;
translations.idiomatic: Shun warned Yu, “Do not be arrogant like Dan Zhu”;

sentences.zh: 周公戒成王，毋若殷王紂。

translations.literal: the Duke of Zhou warned King Cheng, “Do not be like King Zhou of Yin.”
translations.idiomatic: Zhou warned Cheng, “Do not be another Zhou of Shang.”

sentences.zh: 《詩》曰「殷監不遠，在夏后之世」，亦言湯以桀為戒也。

translations.literal: The *Odes* says, “Yin’s mirror is not far—it is in the Xia king’s generation”; this also means Tang took Jie as warning.
translations.idiomatic: The *Odes* says Yin’s lesson is near—in Xia’s fall; Tang took Jie as mirror.

sentences.zh: 聖帝明王常以敗亂自戒，不諱廢興，故臣敢極陳其愚，唯陛下留神察焉。

translations.literal: Sage emperors and bright kings always took defeat and disorder as warning to themselves, not hiding from decline and rise; therefore your servant dares to exhaust his foolishness—only may Your Majesty fix your spirit and examine it.
translations.idiomatic: Wise kings study ruin without shame—so I dare speak bluntly; please weigh every word.

sentences.zh: 謹案春秋二百四十二年，日蝕三十六，襄公尤數，率三歲五月有奇而壹食。

translations.literal: Your servant respectfully checks: in the *Spring and Autumn*’s two hundred forty-two years there were thirty-six solar eclipses; Duke Xiang’s time had the most, on average one eclipse every three years and a bit over five months.
translations.idiomatic: Two hundred forty-two years of the classic hold thirty-six eclipses—most under Duke Xiang, about one every three years five months.

sentences.zh: 漢興訖竟寧，孝景帝尤數，率三歲一月而一食。

translations.literal: From Han’s rise down to Jingning, Emperor Xiaojing had the most, on average one eclipse every three years and a bit over one month.
translations.idiomatic: From Han’s rise to Jingning Jingdi’s reign saw the most—about one eclipse every three years one month.

sentences.zh: 臣向前數言日當食，今連三年比食。

translations.literal: Your servant Xiang earlier repeatedly said the sun would be eclipsed; now for three successive years eclipses have come in succession.
translations.idiomatic: I predicted eclipses; three years running the sun was eaten.

sentences.zh: 自建始以來，二十歲間而八食，率二歲六月而一發，古今罕有。

translations.literal: From Jianshi’s beginning, in twenty years there have been eight eclipses, on average one outbreak every two years and six months—rare in ancient and modern times.
translations.idiomatic: Since Jianshi, twenty years, eight eclipses—one every two years six months, almost unheard of.

sentences.zh: 異有小大希稠，占有舒疾緩急，而聖人所以斷疑也。

translations.literal: Anomalies differ in size, rarity, and density; prognostication has slack and tight, slow and fast—and this is how sages cut through doubt.
translations.idiomatic: Omens differ in scale and pace; diviners read slow or fast—that is how sages cut doubt.

sentences.zh: 《易》曰：「觀乎天文，以察時變。」

translations.literal: The *Changes* says, “Observe the patterns of Heaven, thereby to inspect the changes of the times.”
translations.idiomatic: The *Changes*: “Read the sky to know the hour.”

sentences.zh: 昔孔子對魯哀公，並言夏桀、殷紂暴虐天下，故曆失則攝提失方，孟陬無紀，此皆易姓之變也。

translations.literal: Formerly Confucius replied to Duke Ai of Lu, together speaking of how Xia Jie and Yin Zhou tyrannized the realm; therefore when the calendar went wrong the Handle of Sheti lost its square, the first month of spring had no reckoning—these were changes of changing surname.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius told Duke Ai how Jie and Zhou ravaged the realm—then calendar failed, seasons wandered—signs of dynastic change.

sentences.zh: 秦始皇之末至二世時，日月薄食，山陵淪亡，辰星出於四孟，太白經天而行，無雲而雷，枉矢夜光，熒惑襲月，谪火燒宮，野禽戲廷，都門內崩，長人見臨洮，石隕于東郡，星孛大角，大角以亡。

translations.literal: From the end of the First Emperor of Qin down to the Second Emperor’s time, sun and moon were eclipsed, hills and mounds sank away, the chronogram star appeared in the four meng beginnings, the Great White passed through the sky and traveled, thunder without clouds, crooked arrow by night with light, Mars encroached on the moon, punishing fire burned palaces, wild fowl sported in the court, the inner capital gate collapsed of itself, a tall man appeared at Lintao, stone fell in Dong commandery, a broom-star blazed at Great Horn, and Great Horn thereafter vanished.
translations.idiomatic: Qin’s end: eclipses, hills fell, stars out of season, Great White crossing sky, thunder without cloud, meteors, Mars touching moon, fires in palace, birds in hall, gates fell, giants at Lintao, meteors in Dong, comet in Great Horn—then the Horn vanished.

sentences.zh: 觀孔子之言，考暴秦之異，天命信可畏也。

translations.literal: Viewing Confucius’s words and examining brutal Qin’s anomalies, Heaven’s mandate is indeed to be feared.
translations.idiomatic: Read Confucius, read Qin’s sky—Heaven’s will is terrible.

sentences.zh: 及項籍之敗，亦孛大角。

translations.literal: When Xiang Ji was defeated, a broom-star also blazed at Great Horn.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu’s fall too brought a comet at Great Horn.

sentences.zh: 漢之入秦，五星聚于東井，得天下之象也。

translations.literal: When Han entered Qin, the five planets gathered at Eastern Well—an image of obtaining the realm.
translations.idiomatic: Han’s entry showed five planets at Eastern Well—the chart of conquest.

sentences.zh: 孝惠時，有雨血，日食於衝，滅光星見之異。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Xiaohui there was rain of blood, a solar eclipse at opposition, and the anomaly of the extinguished light star appearing.
translations.idiomatic: Huidi saw blood rain, eclipse at opposition, and the “extinguished light” star.

sentences.zh: 孝昭時，有泰山臥石自立，上林僵柳復起，大星如月西行，眾星隨之，此為特異。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Xiaozhao there was a Taishan boulder that stood up of itself, a dead willow in Shanglin that rose again, a great star like the moon traveling west with many stars following it—this was especially strange.
translations.idiomatic: Zhaodi: standing stone, risen willow, moon-sized star sailing west with a train—uncanny.

sentences.zh: 孝宣興起之表，天狗夾漢而西，久陰不雨者二十餘日，昌邑不終之異也。

translations.literal: The portent of Emperor Xiaoxuan’s rise: the celestial dog flanked the River of Heaven and went west; for more than twenty days there was long overcast without rain—the anomaly of King Changyi’s not finishing his reign.
translations.idiomatic: Xuandi’s omen: the “celestial dog” flanking the Milky Way westward, twenty days of gloom—Changyi’s short throne.

sentences.zh: 皆著於漢紀。

translations.literal: All are recorded in Han annals.
translations.idiomatic: All sit in the Han record.

sentences.zh: 觀秦、漢之易世，覽惠、昭之無後，察昌邑之不終，視孝宣之紹起，天之去就，豈不昭昭然哉！

translations.literal: Observe Qin and Han’s change of mandate, look at Hui and Zhao’s lack of heirs, examine Changyi’s not finishing, view Emperor Xiaoxuan’s succession from rise—Heaven’s going and staying, is it not blazing plain!
translations.idiomatic: Read Qin to Han, Hui and Zhao childless, Changyi cut short, Xuandi’s rise—Heaven’s choice is written plain.

sentences.zh: 高宗、成王亦有雊雉拔木之變，能思其故，故高宗有百年之福，成王有復風之報。

translations.literal: High Zong and King Cheng also had the anomalies of crowing pheasants and uprooted trees; able to ponder their causes, therefore High Zong had a hundred years’ blessing, and King Cheng had the response of the winds returning.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozong and Cheng had pheasants and torn trees too; they thought and won long life and the gale’s return.

sentences.zh: 神明之應，應若景嚮，世所同聞也。

translations.literal: The spiritual luminaries’ responses answer like shadow and echo—what the age has heard together.
translations.idiomatic: Spirit answers echo shadow—every age knows it.

sentences.zh: 臣幸得託末屬，誠見陛下有寬明之德，冀銷大異，而興高宗、成王之聲，以崇劉氏，故豤豤數奸死亡之誅。

translations.literal: Your servant, fortunate to be a distant twig of the lineage, truly sees that Your Majesty has a broad and clear virtue; he hopes to melt the great anomalies and raise the fame of High Zong and King Cheng, thereby to exalt the Liu house; therefore earnestly and repeatedly he has braved the death penalty.
translations.idiomatic: I am a twig of Liu, I see your virtue, I beg to quell these signs as Gaozong and Cheng did—so I risk execution again and again.

sentences.zh: 今日食尤屢，星孛東井，攝提炎及紫宮，有識長老莫不震動，此變之大者也。

translations.literal: Now solar eclipses are especially frequent; a broom-star blazes at Eastern Well; Sheti’s blaze reaches Purple Palace; elders of discernment are all shaken—this is among the greatest of changes.
translations.idiomatic: Eclipses stack; comet in Eastern Well; Sheti’s fire licks Ziwei—every elder trembles: a chief prodigy.

sentences.zh: 其事難一二記，故《易》曰「書不盡言，言不盡意」，是以設卦指爻，而復說義。

translations.literal: The affairs are hard to record one by two; therefore the *Changes* says, “Writing does not exhaust speech, speech does not exhaust meaning”—therefore it sets hexagrams and points lines, then again expounds the meaning.
translations.idiomatic: The tale is too large for ink; the *Changes* says words fail meaning—hence lines and images.

sentences.zh: 《書》曰「伻來以圖」，天文難以相曉，臣雖圖上，猶須口說，然後可知，願賜清燕之閒，指圖陳狀。

translations.literal: The *Documents* says, “Come with charts”; astronomy is hard to make clear in mutual understanding; though your servant charts upward, still oral explanation is needed before it can be understood—he begs a space of clear leisure to point at charts and set forth the forms.
translations.idiomatic: The *Documents* says “bring maps”; sky lore needs a voice as well as charts—grant me quiet time to unfold them.

sentences.zh: 上輒入之，然終不能用也。

translations.literal: The sovereign always took him in, yet in the end could not employ him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor always heard him out yet never acted.

sentences.zh: 向每召見，數言公族者國之枝葉，枝葉落則本根無所庇廕；

translations.literal: Whenever Xiang was summoned to audience, he repeatedly said the ducal house is the state’s branches and leaves; when branches and leaves fall, the root has no shelter;
translations.idiomatic: Each summons he said Liu kin are the tree’s leaves—strip the leaves and the trunk roasts;

sentences.zh: 方今同姓疏遠，母黨專政，祿去公室，權在外家，非所以彊漢宗，卑私門，保守社稷，安固後嗣也。

translations.literal: now those of the same surname are distant, the mother’s party monopolizes government, salary has left the ducal hall, power lies with the affinal house—this is not the way to strengthen the Han lineage, humble private gates, guard altars and soil, and secure posterity.
translations.idiomatic: today kin are far, affines rule, pay leaves the public hall—no way to steel the Liu line, humble private gates, guard the shrines, or save your heirs.

sentences.zh: 向自見得信於上，故常顯訟宗室，譏刺王氏及在位大臣，其言多痛切，發於至誠。

translations.literal: Xiang, seeing himself trusted by the sovereign, therefore constantly spoke openly for the imperial clan, satirizing the Wang surname and great ministers in office; his words were mostly bitter and cutting, issuing from utmost sincerity.
translations.idiomatic: Trusted, he scolded Wangs and ministers in office—his words cut because his heart was true.

sentences.zh: 上數欲用向為九卿，輒不為王氏居位者及丞相御史所持，故終不遷。

translations.literal: The sovereign several times wished to employ Xiang among the nine ministers, yet each time those of the Wang surname in place and the chancellor and imperial clerk held him back, so in the end he was not promoted.
translations.idiomatic: The throne meant to raise him to nine ministers; Wangs in seat, chancellor, and censor blocked him—he never rose.

sentences.zh: 居列大夫官前後三十餘年，年七十二卒。

translations.literal: He held rank among the grandee officials for more than thirty years forward and back, and died at age seventy-two.
translations.idiomatic: He served as grandee thirty-odd years and died at seventy-two.

sentences.zh: 卒後十三歲而王氏代漢。

translations.literal: Thirteen years after his death the Wang surname replaced Han.
translations.idiomatic: Thirteen years later Wang Mang took the throne.

sentences.zh: 向三子皆好學：長子伋，以易教授，官至郡守；

translations.literal: Xiang’s three sons all loved learning: the eldest son Ji, who taught the *Changes* and rose to grand administrator of a commandery;
translations.idiomatic: Three sons, all scholars: Ji taught *Changes* to commandery governor;

sentences.zh: 中子賜，九卿丞，蚤卒；

translations.literal: the middle son Ci, assistant among the nine ministers, died early;
translations.idiomatic: Ci, nine-minister aide, died young;

sentences.zh: 少子歆，最知名。

translations.literal: the youngest son Xin, most renowned.
translations.idiomatic: Xin, the famous one.

sentences.zh: 歆字子駿，少以通詩書能屬文召見成帝，待詔宦者署，為黃門郎。

translations.literal: Xin, styled Zijun, in his youth for thorough knowledge of the *Odes* and *Documents* and ability to compose texts was summoned to audience by Emperor Cheng and awaited edicts at the eunuchs’ office, serving as gentleman at the Yellow Gate.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xin, style Zijun, read the classics, wrote well, met Chengdi, waited edicts with the eunuchs, became gentleman at the Yellow Gate.

sentences.zh: 河平中，受詔與父向領校祕書，講六藝傳記，諸子、詩賦、數術、方技，無所不究。

translations.literal: In the Heping period he received an edict together with his father Xiang to direct collation of the secretariat books, expounding the six arts, transmissions and records, masters, *Odes* and *fu*, numerology and technical arts—investigating all without omission.
translations.idiomatic: Heping: he and his father collated palace books—six arts, histories, masters, rhapsodies, math, medicine—nothing left out.

sentences.zh: 向死後，歆復為中壘校尉。

translations.literal: After Xiang died, Xin again became colonel of the garrisoned encampment.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiang died Xin took the colonel’s baton again.

sentences.zh: 哀帝初即位，大司馬王莽舉歆宗室有材行，為侍中太中大夫，遷騎都尉、奉車光祿大夫，貴幸。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ai first took the throne, grand marshal Wang Mang recommended Xin as imperial clansman with talent and conduct, and he became attendant within and grand counsellor of the palace; he was promoted cavalry commandant and grand counsellor of the palace serving as chariot conductor—honored and favored.
translations.idiomatic: Aidi’s accession: Wang Mang praised him; Xin rose to attendant, grand counsellor, cavalry commandant, chariot conductor—high favor.

sentences.zh: 復領五經，卒父前業。

translations.literal: Again he directed the five classics and completed his father’s former work.
translations.idiomatic: He finished the five classics and his father’s work.

sentences.zh: 歆乃集六藝群書，種別為七略。

translations.literal: Xin thereupon collected the six arts’ mass of books, sorting them by kind into seven summaries.
translations.idiomatic: He sorted all texts into the *Seven Summaries*.

sentences.zh: 語在藝文志。

translations.literal: The account is in the “Treatise on Literature and the Arts.”
translations.idiomatic: See the bibliographic treatise.

sentences.zh: 歆及向始皆治易，宣帝時，詔向受穀梁春秋，十餘年，大明習。

translations.literal: Xin and Xiang at first both studied the *Changes*; under Emperor Xuan an edict ordered Xiang to receive the Guliang *Spring and Autumn*; after more than ten years he was greatly enlightened and practiced.
translations.idiomatic: Father and son began with *Changes*; Xuandi set Xiang to Guliang for ten years until he mastered it.

sentences.zh: 及歆校秘書，見古文春秋左氏傳，歆大好之。

translations.literal: When Xin collated secretariat books, he saw the old-text *Zuo Tradition* to the *Spring and Autumn* and greatly loved it.
translations.idiomatic: Collating, he found the old-text *Zuo* and loved it.

sentences.zh: 時丞相史尹咸以能治左氏，與歆共校經傳。

translations.literal: At that time the clerk to the chancellor, Yin Xian, because he could treat the Zuo tradition, together with Xin jointly collated the classic and commentary.
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor’s clerk Yin Xian knew *Zuo*; they collated together.

sentences.zh: 歆略從咸及丞相翟方進受，質問大義。

translations.literal: Xin broadly followed Xian and received from Chancellor Zhai Fangjin as well, questioning the great meanings.
translations.idiomatic: Xin studied with Yin and Zhai Fangjin on the great points.

sentences.zh: 初左氏傳多古字古言，學者傳訓故而已，及歆治左氏，引傳文以解經，轉相發明，由是章句義理備焉。

translations.literal: At first the Zuo tradition had many ancient graphs and ancient words; scholars transmitted glosses and explanations only; when Xin treated the Zuo tradition, he cited commentary text to explicate the classic, mutually bringing out meaning, whereupon chapter-and-sentence meaning was complete.
translations.idiomatic: Early *Zuo* was dense archaism; scholars glossed lines; Xin used the commentary to unlock the classic until doctrine stood whole.

sentences.zh: 歆亦湛靖有謀，父子俱好古，博見彊志，過絕於人。

translations.literal: Xin was also deep, quiet, and resourceful; father and son both loved antiquity, saw broadly and remembered strongly, surpassing others.
translations.idiomatic: Xin was deep, still, clever; both loved antiquity, read wide, forgot nothing, outshone peers.

sentences.zh: 歆以為左丘明好惡與聖人同，親見夫子，而公羊、穀梁在七十子後，傳聞之與親見之，其詳略不同。

translations.literal: Xin thought Zuo Qiuming’s likes and dislikes matched the sage’s; he had personally seen the Master, while Gongyang and Guliang came after the seventy disciples—between hearing by transmission and seeing in person, their detail and summary differ.
translations.idiomatic: Xin held that Zuo Qiu saw Confucius while Gongyang and Guliang heard disciples—sight beats rumor for detail.

sentences.zh: 歆數以難向，向不能非間也，然猶自持其穀梁義。

translations.literal: Xin repeatedly used this to challenge Xiang; Xiang could not refute him, yet still held to his Guliang meaning.
translations.idiomatic: Xin pressed his father; Xiang could not rebut yet clung to Guliang.

sentences.zh: 及歆親近，欲建立左氏春秋及毛詩、逸禮、古文尚書皆列於學官。

translations.literal: When Xin grew intimate, he wished to establish the Zuo *Spring and Autumn* and the Mao *Odes*, the lost *Rites*, and the old-text *Documents* all in the academy.
translations.idiomatic: In favor, he asked the throne to put *Zuo*, Mao *Odes*, lost *Rites*, and old-text *Documents* in the academy.

sentences.zh: 哀帝令歆與五經博士講論其義，諸博士或不肯置對，歆因移書太常博士，責讓之曰：

translations.literal: Emperor Ai ordered Xin to debate their meaning with the five-classics erudites; some of the erudites would not consent to answer; Xin therefore sent a letter to the grand erudites of the grand master of ceremonies, reproaching them, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Aidi made him debate the doctors; some refused; Xin wrote the famous letter scolding the grand erudites:

sentences.zh: 昔唐虞既衰，而三代迭興，聖帝明王，累起相襲，其道甚著。

translations.literal: Formerly, when Tang and Yu had declined and the three ages rose in turn, sage emperors and bright kings arose in succession, one after another; their way was very manifest.
translations.idiomatic: When Tang and Yu faded, three dynasties rose in turn—sage kings stacked bright example.

sentences.zh: 周室既微而禮樂不正，道之難全也如此。

translations.literal: The Zhou house had already grown faint and ritual and music were not correct—how hard it is to keep the Way whole is like this.
translations.idiomatic: Late Zhou broke rites and music—how hard it is to keep the whole Way.

sentences.zh: 是故孔子憂道之不行，歷國應聘。

translations.literal: Therefore Confucius grieved that the Way would not go; he traveled the states answering invitations.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius mourned the Way and wandered seeking a throne.

sentences.zh: 自衛反魯，然後樂正，雅頌乃得其所；

translations.literal: Returning from Wei to Lu, then music was corrected, and the Ya and Song came to their proper places;
translations.idiomatic: From Wei to Lu he fixed music until Ya and Song sat right;

sentences.zh: 修易，序書，制作春秋，以紀帝王之道。

translations.literal: he edited the *Changes*, arranged the *Documents*, composed the *Spring and Autumn* to record the way of emperors and kings.
translations.idiomatic: he ordered *Changes* and *Documents* and cut the *Spring and Autumn* for kings’ law.

sentences.zh: 及夫子沒而微言絕，七十子終而大義乖。

translations.literal: When the Master died subtle words ceased; when the seventy disciples ended, great meanings went awry.
translations.idiomatic: When the Master died subtle speech died; when the seventy passed, doctrine wandered.

sentences.zh: 重遭戰國，棄籩豆之禮，理軍旅之陳，孔氏之道抑，而孫吳之術興。

translations.literal: Again meeting the Warring States period, they cast aside the rites of baskets and tuns, arranged the formations of armies—Confucius’s way was suppressed, while the arts of Sun and Wu rose.
translations.idiomatic: Warring States scrapped ritual for battle lines—Confucius sank, Sun and Wu rose.

sentences.zh: 陵夷至于暴秦，燔經書，殺儒士，設挾書之法，行是古之罪，道術由是遂滅。

translations.literal: Declining down to brutal Qin, they burned the classics and killed Confucians, established the law against keeping books, punished those who praised antiquity—by this the arts of the Way were extinguished.
translations.idiomatic: Qin burned books, killed scholars, banned private libraries, punished “praising antiquity”—and the Way went dark.

sentences.zh: 漢興，去聖帝明王遐遠，仲尼之道又絕，法度無所因襲。

translations.literal: When Han arose, it was far removed from sage emperors and bright kings; Confucius’s way was again cut off; laws and standards had nothing to inherit.
translations.idiomatic: Han woke far from the sages; Confucius’ thread was snapped; law had no model.

sentences.zh: 時獨有一叔孫通略定禮儀，天下唯有易卜，未有它書。

translations.literal: At that time only Shusun Tong roughly fixed ritual forms; under Heaven there was only *Changes* divination, no other books.
translations.idiomatic: Only Shusun Tong sketched court ritual; the realm had *Changes* oracles and little else.

sentences.zh: 至孝惠之世，乃除挾書之律，然公卿大臣絳、灌之屬咸介冑武夫，莫以為意。

translations.literal: Down to Emperor Xiaohui’s age, the law against keeping books was repealed, yet dukes and ministers—the Jiang and Guan class—were all armored warriors and paid no heed.
translations.idiomatic: Huidi lifted the book ban, yet Jiang, Guan, and peers were soldiers in mail who shrugged at books.

sentences.zh: 至孝文皇帝，始使掌故晁錯從伏生受尚書。

translations.literal: Down to Emperor Xiaowen, he first had the clerk Chao Cuo receive the *Documents* from Master Fu.
translations.idiomatic: Wendi first sent clerk Chao Cuo to Fu Sheng for the *Documents*.

sentences.zh: 尚書初出于屋壁，朽折散絕，今其書見在，時師傳讀而已。

translations.literal: The *Documents* first came out from a house wall, rotten, broken, and scattered apart; now the book exists, and masters of the time transmit and read it only.
translations.idiomatic: The *Documents* crawled from a wall, moldy and split; today’s teachers only mouth the scraps.

sentences.zh: 詩始萌牙。

translations.literal: The *Odes* first sprouted.
translations.idiomatic: The *Odes* barely stirred.

sentences.zh: 天下眾書往往頗出，皆諸子傳說，猶廣立於學官，為置博士。

translations.literal: Books under Heaven often appeared in considerable numbers; all were masters’ explanations, yet they were broadly set up in the academy and erudites were appointed for them.
translations.idiomatic: Texts surfaced—mostly master’s glosses—yet the court opened chairs and named doctors.

sentences.zh: 在漢朝之儒，唯賈生而已。

translations.literal: Among Han’s Confucians there was only Master Jia.
translations.idiomatic: Han’s first true scholar was Jia Yi.

sentences.zh: 至孝武皇帝，然後鄒、魯、梁、趙頗有詩、禮、春秋先師，皆起於建元之間。

translations.literal: Down to Emperor Xiaowu, then in Zou, Lu, Liang, and Zhao there were somewhat early masters of the *Odes*, *Rites*, and *Spring and Autumn*—all arose between the Jianyuan years.
translations.idiomatic: Wudi drew teachers of *Odes*, *Rites*, and *Spring and Autumn* from Zou, Lu, Liang, and Zhao—the Jianyuan generation.

sentences.zh: 當此之時，一人不能獨盡其經，或為雅，或為頌，相合而成。

translations.literal: At that time one man could not exhaust a whole classic alone; some did Ya, some Song—combining to complete it.
translations.idiomatic: No one held a whole classic—some taught Ya, some Song—patching a canon together.

sentences.zh: 泰誓後得，博士集而讀之。

translations.literal: The “Great Oath” came later; erudites assembled and read it.
translations.idiomatic: The *Great Oath* arrived late; doctors read it in council.

sentences.zh: 故詔書稱曰：「禮壞樂崩，書缺簡脫，朕甚閔焉。」

translations.literal: Therefore the edict said, “Rites are broken, music collapsed, documents are missing and slips are lost—We are deeply grieved.”
translations.idiomatic: The edict mourned “broken rites, lost music, missing slips.”

sentences.zh: 時漢興已七八十年，離於全經，固已遠矣。

translations.literal: By then Han had risen seventy or eighty years—distance from a complete classic was indeed far.
translations.idiomatic: Eight decades in, a full text was still a distant hope.

sentences.zh: 及魯恭王壞孔子宅，欲以為宮，而得古文於壞壁之中，逸禮有三十九，書十六篇。

translations.literal: When King Gong of Lu destroyed Confucius’s house, intending to make it a palace, he obtained old-text documents in the broken walls—thirty-nine chapters of lost *Rites*, sixteen chapters of *Documents*.
translations.idiomatic: King Gong of Lu tore Confucius’ house for a palace and found old texts in the walls—thirty-nine lost *Rites* chapters, sixteen of *Documents*.

sentences.zh: 天漢之後，孔安國獻之，遭巫蠱倉卒之難，未及施行。

translations.literal: After Tianhan, Kong Anguo presented them, but met the witchcraft turmoil in haste and had no time to implement them.
translations.idiomatic: After Tianhan Kong Anguo offered them; witchcraft chaos blocked adoption.

sentences.zh: 及春秋左氏丘明所修，皆古文舊書，多者二十餘通，臧於祕府，伏而未發。

translations.literal: Down to the Zuo *Spring and Autumn* edited by Qiu Ming—all were old-text ancient books, in many cases more than twenty scrolls, stored in the secret vault, hidden and not promulgated.
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo *Spring and Autumn* by Qiu Ming—old script, twenty-odd scrolls—slept in the palace vault.

sentences.zh: 孝成皇帝閔學殘文缺，稍離其真，乃陳發祕臧，校理舊文，得此三事，以考學官所傳，經或脫簡，傳或間編。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaocheng pitied learning’s mutilated texts and missing passages, gradually departing from truth; therefore he opened the secret store, collated old texts, and obtained these three matters to check what the academy transmitted—the classic sometimes lacked slips, the commentary sometimes had gaps in arrangement.
translations.idiomatic: Chengdi pitied broken learning, opened the vault, collated, and found three bodies of text that showed academy copies missing lines and jumbled chapters.

sentences.zh: 傳問民間，則有魯國桓公、趙國貫公、膠東庸生之遺學與此同，抑而未施。

translations.literal: Inquiring in the common folk, there were the surviving studies of Huan Gong of Lu, Guan Gong of Zhao, and Master Yong of Jiaodong matching these, yet suppressed and not applied.
translations.idiomatic: In the villages the same text lived with Huan Gong of Lu, Guan Gong of Zhao, and Yong of Jiaodong—yet stayed buried.

sentences.zh: 此乃有識者之所惜閔，士君子之所嗟痛也。

translations.literal: This is what men of insight pity and grieve, what gentlemen sigh and lament.
translations.idiomatic: Wise men mourn it; gentlemen ache.

sentences.zh: 往者綴學之士不思廢絕之闕，苟因陋就寡，分文析字，煩言碎辭，學者罷老且不能究其一藝。

translations.literal: Formerly patchwork scholars did not think of the gaps of extinction and ruin, but were content with meagerness and paucity, splitting graphs and parsing words, tiresome speech and fragmentary phrases—students grew old and still could not exhaust one art.
translations.idiomatic: Pedants hugged scraps, split characters, spun chatter—students grayed without mastering one school.

sentences.zh: 信口說而背傳記，是末師而非往古，至於國家將有大事，若立辟雍封禪巡狩之儀，則幽冥而莫知其原。

translations.literal: They trusted oral sayings and turned their backs on transmitted records, honored last teachers and rejected antiquity; when the state faced great matters—such as establishing a Biyong, feng and shan sacrifices, or hunting-tour rites—they were dark and ignorant of the origins.
translations.idiomatic: They favored mouth over manuscript; when the court needed Biyong, feng-shan, or hunt rites, they groped in the dark.

sentences.zh: 猶欲保殘守缺，挾恐見破之私意，而無從善服義之公心，或懷妒嫉，不考情實，雷同相從，隨聲是非，抑此三學，以尚書為備，謂左氏為不傳春秋，豈不哀哉！

translations.literal: Yet they still wished to protect the damaged and guard the missing, hugging private fear of being exposed, without the public mind to follow the good and submit to right; some harbored jealousy, did not examine the real situation, echoed one another following sound, affirming and denying at random—suppressing these three studies, holding the transmitted *Documents* complete, calling the Zuo tradition “not a transmission of the *Spring and Autumn*”—is it not pitiable!
translations.idiomatic: They clung to shards, feared exposure, envied truth, mobbed the same tune, damned *Zuo* and Mao while calling the current *Documents* whole—pitiful!

sentences.zh: 今聖上德通神明，繼統揚業，亦閔文學錯亂，學士若茲，雖昭其情，猶依違謙讓，樂與士君子同之。

translations.literal: Now the sage sovereign’s virtue penetrates the spirits; continuing the line and raising the enterprise, he also pities the confusion of letters and learning, scholars like this; though he has clarified the situation, he still hesitates in modest yielding, happy to share the matter with gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: Today’s throne reads the tangle and still asks humbly to share the burden with gentlemen.

sentences.zh: 故下明詔，試左氏可立不，遣近臣奉指銜命，將以輔弱扶微，與二三君子比意同力，冀得廢遺。

translations.literal: Therefore he issued a clear edict to test whether the Zuo tradition may be established, dispatching intimate ministers bearing command to assist the weak and support the subtle, with two or three gentlemen joining mind and strength, hoping to recover what was cast aside.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered a trial for *Zuo* in court, sent close ministers to nurse the nearly lost, and asked a few of you to help.

sentences.zh: 今則不然，深閉固距，而不肯試，猥以不誦絕之，欲以杜塞餘道，絕滅微學。

translations.literal: Now it is not so: deeply closed and firmly barred, unwilling to test, vulgarly cutting it off because it is not recited—wishing thereby to block remaining paths and extinguish subtle learning.
translations.idiomatic: Instead you slam the gate, refuse the test, ban what you have not memorized—choking every side path.

sentences.zh: 夫可與樂成，難與慮始，此乃眾庶之所為耳，非所望士君子也。

translations.literal: One may share joy in a finished deed, but it is hard to plan the beginning with him—this is the common crowd’s conduct, not what is expected of gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: Crowds toast the finish but fear the first step—that is mob work, not a gentleman’s.

sentences.zh: 且此數家之事，皆先帝所親論，今上所考視，其古文舊書，皆有徵驗，外內相應，豈苟而已哉！

translations.literal: Moreover these several houses’ matters were all personally discussed by former emperors and examined by the present sovereign; their old-text ancient books all have verification, inner and outer matching—how could it be careless!
translations.idiomatic: These texts were weighed by Xuandi and Chengdi; inner and outer evidence match—this is no whim.

sentences.zh: 夫禮失求之於野，古文不猶愈於野乎？

translations.literal: When rites are lost, seek them in the countryside—are not old texts better than the countryside?
translations.idiomatic: When court ritual fails, search the fields—is old script not richer than rumor?

sentences.zh: 往者博士書有歐陽，春秋公羊，易則施、孟，然孝宣皇帝猶復廣立穀梁春秋，梁丘易，大小夏侯尚書，義雖相反，猶並置之。

translations.literal: Formerly among erudites there were Ouyang’s *Documents*, Gongyang’s *Spring and Autumn*, and in the *Changes* Shi and Meng—yet Emperor Xiaoxuan still broadly established Guliang *Spring and Autumn*, Liangqiu *Changes*, and great and small Xiahou *Documents*; though meanings opposed one another, still all were set up.
translations.idiomatic: Xuandi once let Gongyang and Guliang, Shi and Liangqiu, Ouyang and Xiahou sit together though they contradicted.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 與其過而廢之也，寧過而立之。

translations.literal: Better err by establishing than err by abolishing.
translations.idiomatic: Better erect too much than tear down too fast.

sentences.zh: 傳曰：「文武之道未墜於地，在人；

translations.literal: The tradition says, “The way of Wen and Wu has not fallen to the ground—it lies in men;
translations.idiomatic: The tradition says, “Zhou’s way still walks among men;

sentences.zh: 賢者志其大者，不賢者志其小者。」

translations.literal: the worthy remember its great parts, the unworthy its small parts.”
translations.idiomatic: the worthy grasp the large, the small man the small.”

sentences.zh: 今此數家之言所以兼包大小之義，豈可偏絕哉！

translations.literal: Now these several houses’ words therefore embrace both great and small meanings—how can they be one-sidedly cut off!
translations.idiomatic: These schools hold both great and small doctrine—how can you choke them?

sentences.zh: 若必專己守殘，黨同門，妒道真，違明詔，失聖意，以陷於文吏之議，甚為二三君子不取也。

translations.literal: If you must cling to yourself and guard the damaged, faction with your gate, envy the true Way, disobey the clear edict, miss the sage’s intent, and fall into the clerks’ indictment—that is very much what two or three gentlemen should not choose.
translations.idiomatic: Cling to one school, envy the true line, defy the edict, miss the sage’s mind, and face indictment—that is unworthy of you.

sentences.zh: 其言甚切，諸儒皆怨恨。

translations.literal: His words were very cutting; the various Confucians all harbored resentment.
translations.idiomatic: The letter cut deep; the doctors hated him.

sentences.zh: 是時名儒光祿大夫龔勝以歆移書上疏深自罪責，願乞骸骨罷。

translations.literal: At that time the famed Confucian, grand counsellor of the palace Gong Sheng, because of Xin’s letter submitted a memorial deeply blaming himself and begged to give back his bones and retire.
translations.idiomatic: Gong Sheng, famed doctor, read the letter, blamed himself, and begged to retire.

sentences.zh: 及儒者師丹為大司空，亦大怒，奏歆改亂舊章，非毀先帝所立。

translations.literal: When the Confucian Shi Dan became grand minister of works, he too was greatly angry and memorialized that Xin had altered and disordered old norms and slandered what the former emperor had established.
translations.idiomatic: Minister Shi Dan raged that Xin rewrote Xuandi’s chairs.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「歆欲廣道術，亦何以為非毀哉？」

translations.literal: The sovereign said, “Xin wished to broaden the arts of the Way—how is that slandering what was established?”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor answered, “He only widened learning—where is the slander?”

sentences.zh: 歆由是忤執政大臣，為眾儒所訕，懼誅，求出補吏，為河內太守。

translations.literal: Xin thereby offended the great ministers in power, was ridiculed by the mass of Confucians, feared execution, begged to go out and fill a clerk post, and became grand administrator of Henei.
translations.idiomatic: Xin angered the bloc, feared the knife, asked for a post, and became governor of Henei.

sentences.zh: 以宗室不宜典三河，徙守五原，後復轉在涿郡，歷三郡守。

translations.literal: Because imperial clansmen were not fit to govern the Three Rivers, he was transferred to guard Wuyuan, later rotated to Zhuo commandery—serving as guardian of three commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: Clansmen could not hold the Three Rivers—he was shifted to Wuyuan, then Zhuo: three governorships.

sentences.zh: 數年，以病免官，起家復為安定屬國都尉。

translations.literal: After several years, on illness he was dismissed from office; rising from commoner status he again became colonel of dependent states at Anding.
translations.idiomatic: Years later illness ended his post; he was recalled as colonel of dependent states at Anding.

sentences.zh: 會哀帝崩，王莽持政，莽少與歆俱為黃門郎，重之，白太后。

translations.literal: It happened that Emperor Ai died; Wang Mang held power; Mang in youth had together with Xin been gentlemen at the Yellow Gate—he valued him and reported to the empress dowager.
translations.idiomatic: Aidi died; Wang Mang, Xin’s old Yellow Gate mate, seized power and praised him to the dowager.

sentences.zh: 太后留歆為右曹太中大夫，遷中壘校尉，羲和，京兆尹，使治明堂辟雍，封紅休侯。

translations.literal: The empress dowager retained Xin as grand counsellor of the palace in the right bureau, promoted him colonel of the garrisoned encampment, Director of Harmonizing Seasons, metropolitan governor, had him administer the Bright Hall and Biyong, and enfeoffed him marquis of Hongxiu.
translations.idiomatic: She kept him as right-bureau grand counsellor, colonel, Director of Harmonizing Seasons, governor of the capital, Bright Hall and Biyong builder, marquis of Hongxiu.

sentences.zh: 典儒林史卜之官，考定律曆，著三統曆譜。

translations.literal: He directed the forest of scholars, scribes, and diviners’ offices, examined and fixed the calendar, and composed the *Harmonizing the Three Successions: Calendar and Genealogy*.
translations.idiomatic: He ran the academy of scholars, fixed the calendar, and wrote the *Triple Concordance* calendar treatise.

sentences.zh: 初，歆以建平元年改名秀，字穎叔云。

translations.literal: Earlier, in the first year of Jianping, Xin changed his name to Xiu, styled Yingshu, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianping first year he renamed himself Xiu, style Yingshu.

sentences.zh: 及王莽篡位，歆為國師，後事皆在莽傳。

translations.literal: When Wang Mang usurped the throne, Xin became state master; later affairs are all in Mang’s biography.
translations.idiomatic: When Mang took the throne Xin became state master—the rest is Mang’s chapter.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：仲尼稱「材難不其然與！」

translations.literal: The appraisal says: Confucius said, “Talent is hard—is it not so!”
translations.idiomatic: Ban Gu’s verdict: Confucius sighed that true talent is rare.

sentences.zh: 自孔子後，綴文之士眾矣，唯孟軻、孫況，董仲舒、司馬遷、劉向、揚雄。

translations.literal: After Confucius, writers who strung texts were many, yet only Meng Ke, Sun Qing, Dong Zhongshu, Sima Qian, Liu Xiang, and Yang Xiong.
translations.idiomatic: After him only Mencius, Xunzi, Dong Zhongshu, Sima Qian, Liu Xiang, and Yang Xiong matter.

sentences.zh: 此數公者，皆博物洽聞，通達古今，其言有補於世。

translations.literal: These several gentlemen broadly comprehended and thoroughly heard, penetrated antiquity and today; their words had supplement for the age.
translations.idiomatic: They read everything, linked past to present, and wrote for the common good.

sentences.zh: 傳曰「聖人不出，其間必有命世者焉」，豈近是乎？

translations.literal: The tradition says, “When no sage appears, among them there must be one who commands the age”—is this not near it?
translations.idiomatic: The saying runs, “Between sages a man still shapes his age”—perhaps these were such.

sentences.zh: 劉氏洪範論發明大傳，著天人之應；

translations.literal: The Liu house’s “Great Plan” treatise expounded the great transmission and set forth Heaven-and-man responses;
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xiang’s *Great Plan* essay joined Heaven and man;

sentences.zh: 七略剖判藝文，總百家之緒；

translations.literal: the *Seven Summaries* dissected literary arts and summed the threads of the hundred schools;
translations.idiomatic: his *Seven Summaries* mapped every school;

sentences.zh: 三統曆譜考步日月五星之度。

translations.literal: the *Triple Concordance* calendar treatise calculated the paces of sun, moon, and five planets.
translations.idiomatic: his *Triple Concordance* fixed the wanderers of sky.

sentences.zh: 有意其推本之也。

translations.literal: One may suppose he pushed to the roots.
translations.idiomatic: He always dug to the root.

sentences.zh: 嗚虖！

translations.literal: Alas!
translations.idiomatic: Alas!

sentences.zh: 向言山陵之戒，于今察之，哀哉！

translations.literal: Xiang’s warning on mounds and tombs—examined today, pitiable!
translations.idiomatic: Xiang’s tomb sermon—read against today—breaks the heart.

sentences.zh: 指明梓柱以推廢興，昭矣！

translations.literal: Pointing to catalpa pillars to infer decline and rise—how bright!
translations.idiomatic: Reading coffin-post omens for rise and fall—how clear!

sentences.zh: 豈非直諒多聞，古之益友與！

translations.literal: Was he not the blunt, trustworthy, wide-hearing friend of antiquity!
translations.idiomatic: Was he not the straight friend the ancients prized!
